chaphead.title: What Is Really Wrong with Global Tax Governance and How to Properly Fix It

author.name:
Tarcisio Diniz Magalhães
Postdoctoral Research Fellow, IBFD. PhD in Law and Justice, Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG). Visiting Researcher, Université McGill (Stikeman Chair in Tax Law), Wirtschaftsuniversität Wien (WU) (Institut für Österreichisches und Internationales Steuerrecht), and Max-Planck-Gesellschaft (Institut für Steuerrecht und Öffentliche Finanzen). Member of the Permanent Commission on Review and Simplification of Tax Legislation of the State of Minas Gerais, Brazil. This article builds on ideas first espoused in T. Magalhães, International Taxation without International Representation? Legality and Legitimacy in Global Tax Governance, in Building Trust in Taxation (B. Peeters, H. Gribnau & J. Badisco eds., Intersentia 2017), and expanded into a PhD Thesis entitled “Critical Theory of International Tax Law”. For helpful support, discussions and suggestions during the development of my arguments, I would like to thank Professors Misabel Derzi, Pasquale Pistone, Onofre Batista Júnior, Allison Christians, Ludmila Oliveira, Wolfgang Schön, Hans Gribnau, Bruno Peeters, Dries Lesage, Joanna Wheeler, Luís Eduardo Schoueri, Ricardo Garcia Antón, Irma Mosquera Valderrama, Tsilly Dagan, Sérgio André Rocha, Fabrício Polido, Rafhael Frattari, Valter Lobato, Stephen Shay, Diane Ring, Peter Dietsch, Thomas Rixen, Cees Peters, as well as researchers at UFMG, McGill, WU and Max Planck, especially Fernanda Alen, Marcela Ribeiro, Marina Marinho, Nathalia Domingues, Thiago Feital, Priscilla César, Ricardo Rodrigues, Pedro Schoueri, Stjepan Gadzo, Andrzej Stasio, Jean-Philippe West, Sriram Govind, Andrés Moreno, Leopoldo Parada. Gratitude also goes to the anonymous peer reviewers and to the participants at the Institute for Global Law & Policy (IGLP) 2018 Conference: “Law in Global Political Economy: Heterodoxy Now”, held at Harvard Law School. This project benefitted from research grants by Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais (FAPEMIG). The author can be contacted at t.magalhaes@ibfd.org.

chaphead.summary: During the course of the last 100 years, the wealthiest and most powerful nations on the planet have systematically gathered around in small groups of experts, scientific committees and working parties, under the auspices of the League of Nations and the OECD, to decide on the appropriate tax policy norms for global implementation. The immediate consequence was, and still is, the creation of an exclusionary architecture that deprives the majority of the world’s countries from meaningfully influencing legal-institutional choices vis-à-vis what countries should tax cross-border transactions, a process that has clear global distributional implications. This article sets off to investigate this process of exclusion. It identifies two central elements that constrain broad participation in global tax governance, engendering the under-representation of the interests of developing countries: expertise and power. As further argued, the international tax arena is better understood as an entrenched political space, where influential actors maintain their privileged positions by dominating the debate and decision-making procedures. The author then proceeds to analyse possible remedies to this cartelistic and bureaucratic club model of international taxation governance, such as the creation of new intergovernmental organizations or forums, concluding that all proposals so far end up reproducing the same top-down technocratic mentality embedded in the work of the League of Nations and the OECD. Rejecting old and new solutions for a just world tax order, what the author believes is actually needed is a completely different approach, grounded on contested multilateral practices and diversity of world views. Using critical legal theory, the goal is to provide an alternative way to think (politically) about fiscal relations among developed and developing states.

p.emph: “Who decides what and how to tax, and for whose benefit?”

footnote.p: P. Dietsch & T. Rixen, Global Tax Governance: What It Is and Why It Matters, in Global Tax Governance: What Is Wrong with It and How to Fix It p. 8 (P. Dietsch & T. Rixen eds., ECPR 2016).

section.title: Introduction
section.p:
Time and time again, the richest and most powerful nations on the planet gather around to decide the fate of global economic and fiscal policymaking.
D. Held, Cosmopolitanism: Ideals and Realities pp. 1-2 (Polity 2010):
Until recently, the West has, by and large, determined the rules of the game on the global stage. During the last century, Western countries presided over a shift in world power – from control via territory to control via the creation of governance structures created in the post-1945 era. From the United Nations Charter and the formation of the Bretton-Woods institutions to the Rio Declaration on the environment and the creation of the World Trade Organization, international agreements have invariably served to entrench a well-established international power structure. The division of the globe into powerful nation-states, with distinctive sets of geopolitical interests, and reflecting the international power structure of 1945, is still embedded in the articles and statutes of leading intergovernmental organizations, such as the IMF and the World Bank. Voting rights are distributed largely in relation to individual financial contributions, and geo-economic strength is integrated into decision-making procedures. The result has been susceptibility of the major international governmental organizations (IGOs) to the agendas of the most powerful states, partiality in enforcement operations (or lack of them altogether), their continued dependency on financial support from a few major states, and weaknesses in the policing of global collective action problems. This has been dominance based on a “club” model of global governance and legitimacy. Policy at the international level has been decided by a core set of powerful countries, above all the “G1”, G5 and G7, with the rest largely excluded from the decision-making process.
As it often happens in international tax law, a small number of rich countries dominate the field, imposing their wills globally through non-binding mechanisms, sometimes referred to as soft power and soft law.
See, e.g., P. Essers, International Tax Justice between Machiavelli and Habermas, 68 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 42 (2014), Journals IBFD, republished in Building Trust in Taxation (B. Peeters, H. Gribnau & J. Badisco eds., Intersentia 2017); C. Peters, On the Legitimacy of International Tax Law (IBFD 2014), Online Books IBFD; I.J.M. Valderrama, Legitimacy and the Making of International Tax Law: The Challenges of Multilateralism, 7 World Tax J. 3 (2015), Journals IBFD; I. Burges & I. Mosquera, Corporate Taxation and BEPS: A Fair Slice for Developing Countries, 1 ELR 29 (2017); I.J.M. Valderrama, Output Legitimacy Deficits and the Inclusive Framework of the OECD/G20 Base Erosion and Profit Shifting Initiative, 72 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 3 (2018), Journals IBFD; R.G. Antón, The 21st Century Multilateralism in International Taxation: The Emperor’s New Clothes?, 8 World Tax J. 2 (2016), Journals IBFD; S.A. Rocha, The Other Side of BEPS: “Imperial Taxation” and the “International Tax Imperialism”, in Tax Sovereignty in the BEPS Era (S.A. Rocha & A. Christians eds., Wolters Kluwer 2017); R.J.S. Tavares, Country-by-Country Reporting? National Sovereignty, International Tax Transparency, and the Inclusive Framework on BEPS, in Tax Sovereignty in the BEPS Era (S.A. Rocha & A. Christians eds., Wolters Kluwer 2017); A. Christians & S. Shay, Assessing BEPS: Origins, Standards, and Responses (IFA Cahiers vol. 102A, 2017), Online Books IBFD; A. Christians & L. van Apeldoorn, The OECD Inclusive Framework, 72 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 4/5 (2018), Journals IBFD. Recently, confronted with tremendous public criticism of the flawed outcomes of their own past policy choices, the same affluent nations now claim that they are capable of, and committed to, meaningful change. Their latest initiative, and arguably one of the most comprehensive and resolute of all, the well-known and much discussed BEPS Action Plan,
See OECD, Addressing Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (OECD 2013), International Organizations’ Documentation IBFD; OECD, Action Plan on Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (OECD 2013), International Organizations’ Documentation IBFD. But see OXFAM, Business Among Friends: Why Corporate Tax Dodgers Are Not Yet Losing Sleep Over Global Tax Reform, OXFAM Briefing Paper 182 (OXFAM 2014); Y. Brauner, Transfer Pricing in BEPS: First Round – Business Interests Win (But, Not in Knock-Out), 43 Intertax 1 (2015). For scholarly assessments of each of the 15 proposed actions, see Y. Brauner, What the BEPS?, 16 Fla. Tax Rev. 2 (2014); Y. Brauner, Treaties in the Aftermath of BEPS, 41 Brook. J. Intl. L. 3 (2016); R.S. Avi-Yonah & H.Xu, Evaluating BEPS: A Reconsideration of the Benefits Principle and Proposal for UN Oversight, 6 Harv. Bus. L. Rev. 2 (2016), short version: R.S. Avi-Yonah & H.Xu, Evaluating BEPS, in Tax Sovereignty in the BEPS Era (S.A. Rocha & A. Christians eds., Wolters Kluwer 2017); A. Christians, BEPS and the New International Tax Order, 2016 BYU L. Rev. (2017). consists of a coordinated global tax reform attempt by the Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development (OECD), a 35-member group of mostly industrialized countries. The announced objective is to stop a global base erosion and profit shifting epidemic, which, however, stems from the very international tax regime (ITR) those countries themselves set up a century ago, and which they made sure would remain as close as possible to its original intent.

As its starting point, this article takes the basic assumption that commitments to substantively changing policies will never be enough without concern for the process itself. As a precondition for the success of any long-term regime, and with regard to the legitimacy of its substantive rules, changes of an inclusive nature need to be made to the institutional background in which policy deliberations are carried out. The main challenge facing international tax governance is not what norms or standards should be implemented across the globe, but the absence of proper venues for all countries, especially developing ones, to fully participate in and influence the process of policymaking.
As such, this article is a (different) response to the double question contained in the subtitle of a recently published compilation of papers: P. Dietsch & T. Rixen eds., Global Tax Governance: What Is Wrong with It and How to Fix It (ECPR 2016). In this age of postnational legal order,
For the concept of postnational law, see N. Krisch, The Case for Pluralism in Postnational Law, in The Worlds of European Constitutionalism (G. de Búrca & J.H.H. Weiler eds., Cambridge University 2012); N. Krisch, Pouvoir Constituant and Pouvoir Irritant in the Postnational Order, 14 Intl. J. Const. L. 3 (2016). participatory legitimacy and accountability must be the defining characteristics of any supranational institution or regime set up to address international taxation issues, embodying the old adage that for so long served to legitimize national tax states: “no taxation without representation”.

Thus, the aim of this article is to offer democratic alternatives to address global fiscal challenges, such as tax competition, base erosion and the global distribution of tax revenues. It starts by showing how and why global tax governance has always evolved through technocratic discourses that were conducted by unaccountable international organizations (IOs) under the control of powerful players. The argument is that what is missing in most analysis is a more radical perspective that takes struggles in the international tax arena seriously,
On the problematic of power, see K. Nielsen, Globalization and Justice p. 282 (Humanity Books 2003): “We need ... to be concerned about attaining or approximating equality in relations of power. We need, in being concerned with that, to be concerned with the kinds of social structures, including modes of production, that place some in positions of dominance and control and place others in positions of submission and powerlessness. We need to see how this works both domestically and internationally. What is crucial to look at is the forms of social organization that produce and sustain relations of power and exploitation”; R. Geuss, Philosophy and Real Politics p. 94 (Princeton University 2008): “A theoretical approach with no place for a theory of power is not merely deeply deficient but actively pernicious, because mystifying”; R. Forst, The Right to Justification: Elements of a Constructivist Theory of Justice p. 258 (J. Flynn trans., Columbia University 2012): “One can say ... that the question of power is the first question of justice. This stresses the need for a theory that focuses not just on justice in the distribution of goods, but on the justice of the ‘basic structure’ of relations of political and economic power, that is, relations of government, of production, and of distribution”. as well as the presence of radical pluralism on the global stage.
See, e.g., N. Krisch, Who is Afraid of Radical Pluralism? Legal Order and Political Stability in the Postnational Space, 24 Ratio Juris 4 (2011), claiming that the legal philosopher Neil MacCormick should not have abandoned his radical pluralist ideas, in favour of an institutional pluralism. To that end, it is necessary to engage with the tradition of critical legal theory, as applied to international law and relations.
Among jurists, Critical Legal Studies (CLS) was an important movement in the United States starting in the 1970s, which also migrated to the field of international law, especially through the work of David Kennedy and Martti Koskenniemi, together with the so-called Third-World Approaches to International Law (TWAIL), the New Approaches to International Law (NAIL), and so on. For the CLS movement, see R.M. Unger, The Critical Legal Studies Movement: Another Time, a Greater Task (2nd ed., Verso 2015). On the many different possible critical approaches to international law, see P. Singh & B. Mayer eds., Critical International Law: Postrealism, Postcolonialism, and Transnationalism (Oxford University 2014); A. Bianchi, International Law Theories: An Inquiry into Different Ways of Thinking (Oxford University 2016). From such a theoretical framework, this article goes on to critically evaluate a set of alternatives to our “club model”
R.O. Keohane & J.S. Nye Jr., The Club Model of Multilateral Cooperation and Problems of Democratic Legitimacy, in Power and Governance in a Partially Globalized World (R.O. Keohane ed., Routledge 2002), describing the democratic deficit of the club model and suggesting steps to improve the legitimacy of international institutions. of global tax governance. These include the creation of a separate, stand-alone “International (World) Tax Organization”, and the establishment of informal “tax cooperation forums” (TCFs), as proposed by policy experts in the past and by philosophers, political scientists and tax scholars in more recent years. However, the conclusion is that none of these proposals provides an adequate response to the identified problems of expertise and power. The importance of both attention and fidelity to fundamental democratic values by countries and institutions that wield (and jealously guard) an outsized influence on international taxation today should not be underestimated. Nonetheless, the author submits that for a legitimate world tax order to become a reality it is pressing to incorporate the ideas of multipolarity and pluralism.

section.title: International Taxation without International Representation: How Our “Unruly World of Tax” Is Part of the Same Old “World of Struggle”
section.p: In his most recent book, “A World of Struggle: How Power, Law, and Expertise Shape Global Political Economy”, David Kennedy sheds new light on how the international institutional and legal order that exists conforms to the manoeuvres of technocrats, in competition with the classical figures of powerful states and global capital. As Kennedy explains, these actors, through their never-ending linguistic and knowledge battles over how to “improve” the structure of the global economy, promote (intentionally or otherwise) a certain division and allocation of power, wealth, status and opportunity among nations:

longquote.p:
In a world where so much is open to debate and conflict is all around us, how can it be so difficult to contest and change the things that matter? Things like the distribution of wealth and opportunity or honor and shame. Or the pattern of environmental destruction. Or the ubiquity of kleptocratic rule. The answer is not a mysterious constitutional settlement, the obscure workings of a disaggregated public hand or global value consensus. The answer lies in the strange alchemy of expertise and struggle through which our world is made and remade. [...] In our world, indeterminate language and uncertain knowledge distribute wealth and power. That is strange – and hard to render visible, let alone contest.
D. Kennedy, A World of Struggle: How Power, Law, and Expertise Shape Global Political Economy pp. 1-2 (Princeton University 2016).

section.p: Although Kennedy’s in-depth analysis does not deal with international tax law, the story his book tells and the picture of law and globalization it paints make even more sense after examining how the ITR was created, how it progressed over the years and is still being shaped today.

This is one of the gaps this article tries to fill: to reveal the alchemy of expertise and power through which international tax law is made and remade. This effort is worthwhile considering that tax scholars have tended to overlook questions of power and the existence of asymmetric and dependency relationships among diverse tax states. One clear example is a recent paper – discussed in section 3.2.2. – that calls our world of tax “unruly”, paving the way for the authors’ proposal on how to turn such a world into a well-ordered one.
See H.D. Rosenbloom, N. Noked & M.S. Helal, The Unruly World of Tax: A Proposal for an International Tax Cooperation Forum, 15 Fla. Tax Rev. 2 (2014). Challenging such a view, this article claims and demonstrates that our tax order is hegemonic and distributionally biased, being governed by a few players.

Therefore, it is better to avoid describing the ITR as if it were a system free of domination and exclusion mechanisms, and focus on confronting the problem of misrepresentation in the international tax arena – which critical theorist Nancy Fraser considers being “the defining issue of the political” [emphasis added].
N. Fraser, Scales of Justice: Reimagining Political Space in a Globalizing World p. 18 (Columbia University 2009); N. Fraser, Fortunes of Feminism: From State-Managed Capitalism to Neoliberal Crisis 196 (2013). Classically, the political was conceptualized by the legal theorist Carl Schmitt as the friend-enemy distinction – which is particularly useful to describe the OECD’s fight against tax havens or multinationals, as responsible for tax evasion, avoidance and base erosion. See C. Schmitt, The Concept of the Political (2nd ed., G. Schwab ed., 2007). When it comes to international taxation, developing countries have been continuously alienated on the basis that they lack the appropriate technical knowledge. But even when such countries possess some expertise, power politics carries the day. This is confirmed by the absence of experts from non-OECD member countries actively participating in decision-making processes on global tax regulation and management.

These two factors – expertise and power, as shapers of international tax law – have a tendency to coexist, so much so that it is impossible to understand one without the other. Being intrinsically interwoven makes it hard to distinguish them in practice.
For some, along the same lines of Michel Foucault’s and Pierre Bourdieu’s philosophical critiques, knowledge and politics can never be completely separated, because one is constitutive of the other. See S. Picciotto, Indeterminacy, Complexity, Technocracy and the Reform of International Corporate Taxation, 24 Social & Legal Studies 2 (2015), applying a Bourdieusian perspective; M. Kilanowski, The Questionable Role of Experts in Global Governance: Beyond David Kennedy Approach, 15 Ekonomia i Prawo. Econ. & Law 4 (2016), applying a Foucaultian perspective. For methodological reasons, however, this article will approach each of them separately.

section.title: Expertise as a source of misrepresentation, or the problem of technocracy
section.p:
Despite its undeniable relevance in modern societies, expert knowledge can pose real obstacles to democratic legitimation of policy decisions through public debate and scrutiny.
See generally L.C. Bresser-Pereira, Technobureaucratic Capitalism (1990) [English translation of a published book in Portuguese], available at http://www.bresserpereira.org.br/BOOKS/1990TechnobureaucraticCapitalism.i.pdf; F. Fischer, Democracy and Expertise: Reorienting Public Inquiry (Oxford University 2009); M.B. Brown, Science in Democracy: Expertise, Institutions, and Representation (The MIT 2009); E. Dargent, Technocracy and Democracy in Latin America: The Experts Running Government (Cambridge University 2015); J. Habermas, The Lure of Technocracy (C. Cronin trans., Polity 2015); P.M. Wood, Technocracy Rising: The Trojan Horse of Global Transformation (Coherent 2015); O.J. Sending, The Politics of Expertise: Competing for Authority in Global Governance (University of Michigan 2015); D.M. Levy & S.J. Peart, Escape from Democracy: The Role of Experts and the Public in Economic Policy (Cambridge University 2017). For an international tax law perspective, see Picciotto, supra n. 14. From the outset, technical discourses in general have the propensity to hide, behind a veil of neutrality and scientificity, normative assumptions about what is right and wrong, what is fair and unfair, while denying the presence of ideologies and entrenched power relations.
See J. Habermas, Toward a Rational Society: Student Protest, Science, and Politics p. 111 (J.J. Shapiro trans., Polity 1970):
Technocratic consciousness is, on the one hand, “less ideological” than all previous ideologies. For it does not have the opaque force of a delusion that only transfigures the implementation of interests. On the other hand today’s dominant, rather glassy background ideology, which makes a fetish of science, is more irresistible and farther-reaching than ideologies of the old type. For with the veiling of practical problems it not only justifies a particular class’s interest in domination and represses another class’s partial need for emancipation, but affects the human race’s emancipatory interest as such.
This is especially true in the case of tax law.
See L. Philipps, Discursive Deficits: A Feminist Perspective on the Power of Technical Knowledge in Fiscal Law and Policy, 11 CJLS/RCDS 1 (1996), short version in Critical Tax Theory: An Introduction (A.C. Infanti & B.J. Crawford eds., Cambridge University 2009), exposing the ways in which technical discourses in the area of fiscal law and policy can be constructed as purely scientific and morally neutral, thus rationalizing social inequalities by making them look “normal”. Expertise also shields political discussions and delegitimizes moral disagreements, excluding from participation and observation those who are deemed non-specialists, even when they will be affected by the outcomes of the policies. Although the fact that tax law is inherently political in nature has never really escaped tax scholars,
A.C. Infanti & B.J. Crawford, Introduction, in Critical Tax Theory: An Introduction p.xxi (A.C. Infanti & B.J. Crawford eds., Cambridge University 2009): “Tax law is political”. they have not always been able to maintain a politically critical view in the face of the technicality of their field.
M.A. Livingston, Radical Scholars, Conservative Field: Putting “Critical Tax Scholarship” in Perspective, 76 N.C. L. Rev. p. 1791 (1998). As a result, international tax scholarship – like its national counterpart
See, e.g., M.A. Livingston, Reinventing Tax Scholarship: Lawyers, Economists, and the Role of the Legal Academy, 83 Cornell L. Rev. 2 (1998), evaluating the state of tax scholarship and making the recommendations that academic lawyers reinvent their field by expanding their methods to include more than economic studies, in dialogue with psychology, political and social sciences; A. Christians et al., Taxation as a Global Socio-Legal Phenomenon, 14 ILSA J. Intl. & Comp. L. 2, p. 303 (2008): “tax scholarship should turn to fields such as international relations, organizational theory, and political philosophy to provide a broader framework for understanding the rapid changes that are taking place in tax policy and politics in the United States and around the world”; D. Ring, The Promise of International Tax Scholarship and Its Implications for Research Design, Theory, and Methodology, 55 St. Louis U. L. J. 307 (2010), proposing that international tax legal scholars expand their methodologies and research agendas, engaging with other fields, like international relations theory. – has been dominated by a “culture of apolitical expert rule”.
The expression comes from Martti Koskenniemi, a critical international legal scholar whose work has sought to bring to the fore how the mainstream managerial approach to international law tends to downplay “the role of will and randomness, passion and ideology in the way the world is governed, and their own implication in it”. M. Koskenniemi, The Politics of International Law pp. 73-74 (Hart 2011).

The political dimension of (tax) law turns out to be even more apparent in the international realm, as international law navigates “between politics and technique”.
Id., at p. 331. See also Kennedy’s pieces, especially D. Kennedy, The Politics of the Invisible College: International Governance and the Politics of Expertise, 5 E.H.R.L.R. (2001); D. Kennedy, Challenging Expert Rule: The Politics of Global Governance, 27 Sydney L. Rev. 5 (2005); Kennedy, supra n. 11, at pp. 1-2. Typically, those who try to cultivate a way of thinking of international law “in apolitical and technical terms” pay the high cost of allowing “expert rule and managerialism, not in competition with politics as in the domestic realm, but as a substitute for it”.
Koskenniemi, supra n. 21, at p. 359. This managerial approach, with its emphasis on informal regimes, transgovernmental networks and soft institutions, pervades international tax reasoning,
See A. Christians, Networks, Norms, and National Tax Policy, 9 Wash. U. Global Stud. L. Rev. 1 (2010). where soft law is extensively used and abused.
D. Ring, Who is Making International Tax Policy? International Organizations as Power Players in a High Stakes World, 33 Fordham Intl. L. J. 649, p. 652 (2010): “despite the formal, hard law power of the state over international tax policy, international organizations influence the actual design of international tax policy and tax rules in a variety of ways, up to and including the creation or exercise of ‘soft law’ power”; S.A. Dean, Neither Rules nor Standards, 87 Notre Dame L. Rev. 2, p. 554 (2011): “Rather than a contest between rules and standards, the dynamics of the international tax regime tend to be viewed in terms of hard and soft law”. For a discussion of the implications of adhering to the soft law grammar in the context of international tax policy, see A. Christians, Hard Law, Soft Law, and International Taxation, 25 Wis. Intl. L. J. 2, p. 332 (2007), claiming that “[i]f the OECD is described as a source of soft law, nations may be expected to comply with its guidance even if they are not legally obligated to do so”. The drawback is that seeing law as a managerial technique results in depoliticizing the ITR,
For a similar line of thought, see M. Koskenniemi, From Apology to Utopia: The Structure of International Legal Argument (Cambridge University 2005); Koskenniemi, supra n. 21, at p. 35 et seq. in turn obscuring underlying imperial relations and patterns of domination. No attention is paid to the exercise of political and economic power by a few players who can shape the rules of the game to their own benefit.

For instance, Reuven Avi-Yonah has relentlessly argued, against Michael Graetz, H. David Rosenbloom, Tsilly Dagan, Julie Roin, Mitchell Kane, Daniel Shaviro and others, that the primary principles of the ITR are so universally accepted that they ought to be viewed as part of customary international law.
See R.S. Avi-Yonah, International Tax as International Law: An Analysis of the International Tax Regime (Cambridge University 2007). Accord Y. Brauner, An International Tax Regime in Crystallization, 56 Tax L. Rev. (2003), republished in International Tax Law, vol. 1 (R.S. Avi-Yonah ed., Edward Elgar 2016). The most significant implication of endorsing such a controversial view is that once the ITR is presented as a “coherent” set of “neutral”
The author thanks Tsilly Dagan for reminding him that one major flaw in this description consists in the fact that coherence does not necessarily imply neutrality. As an example, she mentions networks that, although built around a compatible standard, can still be used to serve and benefit its owners/initiators. principles for defining and dividing the global tax base, countries are said to be constrained in choosing otherwise.
R.S. Avi-Yonah, Comment, 53 Tax L. Rev. pp. 169-170 (2000): “I would argue that the freedom of most countries to adopt international tax rules is severely constrained, even before entering into any tax treaties, by the need to adapt to generally accepted principles of international taxation”. Going even further, another tax scholar, Eduardo Baistrocchi, has recently advanced the idea that the ITR is nothing more than a “legal technology designed by the League of Nations in the 1920s” and “encapsulated in the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development Model Tax Convention on Income and on Capital (OECD Model)”.
E.A. Baistrocchi, The International Tax Regime and the BRIC World: Elements for a Theory, 33 Oxford J. Legal Stud. 4, p. 734 (2013). Particularly, Baistrocchi portrays the process of tax norm emulation concerning transfer pricing rules among the emerging economies of BRICs (Brazil, Russia, India and China) as part of an evolutionary and linear path to global convergence towards technical progress.

These views implicitly suggest that countries that are considered underdeveloped need to catch up with developed ones by constructing tax systems that are similar to the dominant model.
This has been the prevailing rationale behind expert-led tax reforms in developing countries for decades and decades. Compare N. Kaldor, Will Underdeveloped Countries Learn to Tax?, Foreign Aff. (1963), with P. Genschel & L. Seelkopf, Did They Learn to Tax? Taxation Trends Outside the OECD, 23 Rev. Int’l Pol. Econ. 2, pp. 317 and 340 (2016):
The question Kaldor really meant to ask was whether underdeveloped countries could learn to tax like advanced Western democracies. He seemed to take more or less for granted that the rich Western democracies of the OECD-area embodied the hopes and ambitions of the developing rest of the world (RoW). In order to catch up with the West economically and socially, developing countries had to adopt Western methods of taxation, so Kaldor reasoned. [...] For Nicholas Kaldor it went without saying that Western tax systems were the role model for the RoW. International organizations model much of their tax policy advice on Western experience partly because they are often still dominated by Western countries and partly because the West is still the largest reservoir of tax policy expertise. And also academics tend to take the tax policies of OECD countries as their point of departure simply because most data are available on it.
For a discussion of tax expertise, see M. Stewart, “Waiting for Consensus of the Experts?” Expert Discourse and the Politics of Tax Reform, Working Paper (University of Melbourne 2005), available at https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=1532645; A.J. Cockfield, Purism and Contextualism within International Tax Law Analysis: How Traditional Analysis Fails Developing Countries, 5 eJournal Tax Res. 2 (2007). These are just a few examples of how experts – in casu, jurists, but the category includes professional lawyers, accountants, economists and even philosophers
Philosophers, like the ones discussed below – see P. Dietsch & T. Rixen, Tax Competition and Global Background Justice, 22 J. Pol. Phil. 2 (2014); P. Dietsch & T. Rixen, Redistribution, Globalisation, and Multi-Level Governance, 1 Moral Phil. & Polit. 1 (2014); D. Bamford, Realising International Justice: To Constrain or to Counter-Incentivise?, 1 Moral Phil. & Pol. 1 (2014) – behave as experts when they put disagreement and political deliberation aside and perform the monological role of enlightened philosopher kings, offering “her (or, more usually, his) own view of what justice consists in, what rights we have, what fair terms of social co-operation would be, and what all of this is based on”. J. Waldron, Law and Disagreement p. 1 (Clarendon 1999). See also H. Kelsen, Platonic Justice, in What is Justice? Justice, Law, and Politics in the Mirror of Science: Collected Essays by Hans Kelsen (Lawbook Exchange 2013): “Since the ruling philosopher has a knowledge of the divine good and is unique in the possession of his secret, he is entirely different from other men. The mass of people, who have no political rights, have no choice but to believe in the wisdom and grace of the ruler”; R. Forst, Justification and Critique: Towards a Critical Theory of Politics pp. 1 and 4 (Ciaran Cronin trans., 2014): “All too often political philosophy continues to live in a pre-democratic era. It accords priority to teleological values which are supposed to ground a just or good social order, where those who are subjected to this order do not feature in it as authors”; R. Rorty, The Priority of Democracy to Philosophy, in Philosophical Papers vol. 1: Objectivity, Relativism, and Truth (Cambridge University 1991), decrying Enlightenment philosophers who believe in the possibility of providing rationalistic and ahistorical justifications for “moral truths” and “right answers”. In the international tax context, Steven Dean uses the Platonic figure of the philosopher king in a somewhat different manner, emphasizing the illusion of economically rational sovereigns that always act in order to maximize national welfare. S.A. Dean, Philosopher Kings and International Tax: A New Approach to Tax Havens, Tax Flight, and International Tax Cooperation, 58 Hastings L. J. 911, p. 965 (2007), commending the abandonment of the philosopher king model in international tax for an integrated theory, as the one developed by Oona Hathaway, which “acknowledges and attempts to make sense of the array of political and economic pressures that drive a nation’s decision-making process”. – can contribute to the enforcement of a specific set of ideas, as if such ideas were neutral and regardless of their distributional implications. Yet these ideas are not politically, economically or socially neutral, nor are they uncontroversial.
See J.E. Stiglitz, The Euro: How a Common Currency Threatens the Future of Europe (W.W. Norton & Company 2016): “certain ideas do serve certain interests, and we should not be surprised that by and large tend to serve the interests of those who make them, even if they use the more abstract ideas to argue for them. This analysis leads to an inevitable conclusion: economics and politics cannot be separated – as much as economists would like them to be”. Instead, they are responsible for how resources are allocated across time and place. It makes a difference whose ideas become dominant and therefore deserving of emulation by others. As Kennedy puts it, “to say the world is covered in law is also to say we are increasingly governed by experts” who “make decisions that affect the wealth, status and power of other people”.
Kennedy, supra n. 22, at p. 6.

Furthermore, even if these authors were correct in their description of the ITR, it cannot be assumed that convergence is necessarily a good thing. Such an uncritical narrative contributes to the maintenance of an unjust status quo.
As the work of Carole Pateman demonstrates, under circumstances of structural domination and subordination, as well as massive inequalities, it is an illusion to talk about meaningful consent. See generally D.I. O’Neill, M.L. Shanley & I.M. Young eds., Illusion of Consent: Engaging with Carole Pateman (The Pennsylvania State University 2008). See also T. Pogge, The Role of International Law in Reproducing Massive Poverty, in The Philosophy of International Law p. 445 (S. Besson & J. Tasioulas eds., Oxford University 2010): “in so far as very poor people do consent, through a meaningfully democratic process, to some global institutional arrangements, the justificatory force of such consent is weakened by their having no other tolerable option, and weakened even further by the fact that their calamitous circumstances are partly due to those whose conduct this consent is meant to justify”. In many cases, when weaker states are denied the chance to wield significant influence over the process of regime formation and its outcomes, they are left with little choice but to follow suit.
See D. Bentley, International Constraints on National Tax Policy, Tax Notes Intl. (2003), describing how international institutions have restricted the scope of fiscal choices available to developing nations and nations in distress; M. Stewart, Global Trajectories of Tax Reform: The Discourse of Tax Reform in Developing and Transition Countries, 44 Harv. Intl. L. J. 1 (2003), short version in Critical Tax Theory: An Introduction (A.C. Infanti & B.J. Crawford eds., Cambridge University 2009), mapping the influence of powerful states, experts and international institutions on national tax systems; A. Christians, Global Trends and Constraints on Tax Policy in the Least Developed Countries, 42 U.B.C. L. Rev. 2, p. 241 (2010): “a series of inter-related global tax trends have indelibly altered the tax policy landscape and created significant constraints on the policy choices available to the least developed countries”. This lack of policy room is compounded when they are co-opted by technical discourses, which is notable in countries with limited administrative capacity due to the lack of human and economic resources.
This tactic is all but new. Historically, wealthier nations have always taken advantage of their privileged position to disseminate ideas, building “expert consensus”, with the help of international institutions under their control. See M. Chossudovsky, The Globalization of Poverty and the New World Order (2nd ed., Global Research 2003); N. Woods, The Globalizers: The IMF, the World Bank and their Borrowers (Cornell University 2006); H.-J. Chang, Bad Samaritans: The Guilty Secrets of Rich Nations and the Threat to Global Prosperity (Random House Business 2007); R. Peet, Unholy Trinity: The IMF, World Bank and WTO (2nd ed., Zed Books 2009). For case studies on the role of external forces in shaping national tax reforms, see M. Stewart, Tax Policy Transfer to Developing Countries: Politics, Institutions and Experts, in Global Debates about Taxation (H. Nehring & F. Schui eds., Palgrave Macmillan 2007), with respect to Ghana; A. Christians, Tax Treaties for Investment and Aid to Sub-Saharan Africa: A Case Study, in The Effect of Treaties on Foreign Direct Investment: Bilateral Investment Treaties, Double Taxation Treaties, and Investment Flows (K.P. Sauvant & L.E. Sachs eds., Oxford University 2009); M.A.M. Derzi & A.M. Moreira, Tax Reform and International Tax Norm Transmission: Case Study of Brazil – Value-Added Taxes, in Separação de Poderes e Efetividade do Sistema Tributário (M.A.M. Derzi ed., Del Rey 2010); L. Ates, Domestic Political Legitimacy of Tax Reform in Developing Countries: A Case Study of Turkey, 30 Wis. Int’l L. J. 3 (2012). For an overview, see A. Christians, Historic, Comparative and Evolutionary Analysis of Tax Systems, in Separação de Poderes e Efetividade do Sistema Tributário (M.A.M. Derzi ed., Del Rey 2010). It is worth noting that this phenomenon is not limited to developing countries, but the difference is that developed countries have the ability to exert influence back. For a case study on Australia, see D. Bentley, Influence from the Shadows: The OECD, the Shape of Domestic Tax Policy and Lessons for Federal Systems, 13 Revenue L. J. 1 (2003). As a result, while no real inclusive platform for legitimate international tax law-making is made available, marginalized states “remain subjects of an invisible hand – not that of the market, but of expertise which denies its politics”.
Kennedy, supra n. 22, at p. 24. See also Fischer, supra n. 15, at pp. 3-4: “It might be one thing if the knowledge elites were able to handily dispose of the myriad of problems that confront contemporary societies. But we have come to learn over the past decades that the experts are themselves incapable of answering many of the pressing questions, at least with sufficient degrees of certainty, not to mention supplying workable solutions. To further dampen enthusiasm for such a politics we have also learned that scientific expertise is not the neutral, objective phenomenon that it has long purported to be. Indeed, professionals have all too often served the ideological function of legitimating decisions made elsewhere by political rather than scientific means”; H.-J. Chang, Economics: The User’s Guide (Bloomsbury 2014): “Expert knowledge is absolutely necessary, but an expert by definition knows well only a narrow field and we cannot expect him or her to make a sound judgement on issues that involve more than one area of life (that is, most issues), balancing off different human needs, material constraints and ethical values. The possession of expert knowledge can sometimes give you a blinkered view. This dose of scepticism about expert knowledge should be applied to all areas of life, not just economics. But it is especially important in economics – a political argument often presented as a science”. Paraphrasing Ha-Joon Chang, international taxation is too important to be left to the pundits alone, whether they are economists, tax experts or even philosophers.
Chang, supra n. 38: “The economy is too important to be left to the professional economists alone”. Or, in the words of another renowned economist, Joseph Stiglitz:

longquote.p:
There is a pretense that there are no trade-offs, and that, accordingly, decision making can be delegated to technocrats, who are assigned to the complex task of finding and managing the best economic system, and who are thought to be better equipped than politicians to make objective decisions. There are, of course, some problems which should be delegated to technocrats – like choosing the best computer system for running the social security system. But delegating the writing of the rules of the economic game to technocrats can be justified only if there is a single best set of rules, one that makes everyone better off than any other set of rules. This is simply not the case; this view is not only wrong, but dangerous. With a few exceptions, there are always trade-offs. The existence of trade-offs means that there are choices to be made. It is only through the political system that those choices can be properly made, which is why it is so important to remedy the global democratic deficit.
J.E. Stiglitz, Making Globalization Work p. 279 (W.W. Norton & Company 2006).

section.p: Seeing what is at stake, this is why it is so important to try to understand how the transnational order – as it exists today – restricts the flow of ideas and to pay attention to the impact such a restriction has on the process, as well as on the outcomes.

section.title: Power as a source of misrepresentation, or the problem of hegemony
section.p:
Contemporary structures of global tax governance are governed by the same international institutions that have been running the world within living memory. These are the Bretton Woods international financial institutions (IFIs), namely the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank (WB), the World Trade Organization (WTO), the United Nations (UN), but, above all, the OECD.
See, e.g., J. Wouters & K. Meuwissen, Global Tax Governance: Work in Progress?, in Human Rights and Taxation in Europe and the World (G. Kofler, M.P. Maduro & P. Pistone eds., IBFD 2011), Online Books IBFD. A complete list of global actors involved with tax issues would also include numerous regional institutions, but these have not had the same impact as the aforementioned. For an elaboration, see Ring, supra n. 25.

Notwithstanding the crucial role played by each of these institutions, it is the OECD that occupies a hegemonic, and for many years undisturbed, position in international taxation. The OECD gained prominence as a producer of international tax policy when the League of Nations was dissolved and replaced by the United Nations.
J.F.A. Jones, Are Tax Treaties Necessary?, 53 Tax L. Rev. p. 2 (1999): “the OECD has taken [the] place [of the League of Nations] and is becoming the world body overseeing tax treaties”. See also S. Jogarajan, Prelude to the International Tax Treaty Network: 1845-1914 Early Tax Treaties and the Conditions for Action, 5 Oxford J. Legal Stud. 4 (2011); S. Jogarajan, Stamp, Seligman and the Drafting of the 1923 Experts’ Report on Double Taxation, 5 World Tax J. 3 (2013), Journals IBFD; L. Latulippe, The Expansion of the Bilateral Tax Treaty Network in the 1990s: The OECD’s Role in the International Tax Coordination, 27 Austl. Tax F. (2012). It has, ever since, worked diligently to remain a hegemon in tax policy, framing global tax narratives and functioning as a de facto world tax organization.
The OECD takes pride in being a “market leader in developing standards and guidelines in the core of International Taxation”. OECD, Current Tax Agenda p. 75 (OECD 2008) (file with the author). See also A.J. Cockfield, The Rise of the OECD as Informal ‘world tax organization’ through National Responses to E-Commerce Tax Challenges, 8 Yale J. L. & Tech. 1, p. 186 (2006), concluding that “[t]he success of these efforts [to promote the taxation of international e-commerce] likely pre-empted national legislative and administrative actions as governments ... were generally content to abide by the OECD views”.

Explanations for the OECD’s hegemony in the tax area point to the intimate relationship and consubstantiality between expertise and power.
As early as 1963, Nicholas Kaldor cautioned that national tax reform is not only a matter of expertise, but hinges on the concrete power of political actors. See Kaldor, supra n. 31, at p. 418:
the advocacy of fiscal reform is not some magic potion that is capable of altering the balance of political power by stealth. No doubt, expert advice on tax reform can be very useful in making men of good will – ministers or officials – conscious of the precise nature of the legislative and administrative changes that are required. But what can actually be accomplished does not depend merely on the individual good will of ministers or on the correct intellectual appreciation of the technical problems involved. It is predominantly a matter of political power.
What he had in mind at that time was the exercise of power within the boundaries of national territories. However, the same can be said in relation to international taxation and fiscal relations among countries: global powers also constraint national tax policy. Although this hegemonic position is partially attributed to the OECD’s unparalleled technical knowledge, its accumulated expertise is the direct result of substantial resources devoted by its wealthy members over the years to the Committee of Fiscal Affairs (CFA) and the Centre for Tax Policy and Administration (CTPA).
The CFA and CTPA are internal agencies that deal specifically with the development and global diffusion of tax standards and guidelines. For an analysis of how these groups develop tax norms, see Christians, supra n. 24, at pp. 14-33. The power of the OECD stems from the fact that it groups the largest and most powerful economies in the world.
D.E. Spencer, The United Nations: A Forum for Global Tax Issues?, 12 N.Z. J. Tax. L. & Pol., p. 226 (2006):
Historically, the OECD has been dominant in international tax issues because its members, the more industrialised countries, have exercised a major role in the world economy, with significant international trade and investment. Therefore, the OECD countries have had a greater interest in setting the rules for international tax issues, and for that purpose have given considerable resources to the OECD Department of Fiscal Affairs, whose technical expertise and studies are highly respected.
See also Brauner, supra n. 27, at p. 263, proposing a harmonized world tax regime, preferably led by the OECD, on the basis that it is “the most appropriate and practical institution around which a tax harmonization effort could be built, due to its expertise and experience in coordinating international tax cooperation”; H.J. Ault, Reflections on the Role of the OECD in Developing International Tax Norms, 34 Brook. J. Intl. L. 3, p. 781 (2009), arguing that “[w]hile the OECD is certainly not a ‘Ruler of the World,’ by taking advantage of its expanded institutional structure and its high technical standards, going forward it seems to be well-placed to contribute to the development of international tax norms”; H.J. Ault, Some Reflections on the OECD and the Sources of International Tax Principles, Tax Notes Intl., p. 1201 (2013), republished in R.S. Avi-Yonah ed., International Tax Law, vol. 2 (Edward Elgar 2016): “If – and that is a big if – countries can agree within the framework of the OECD on what kinds of tax provisions are the legitimate exercise of tax sovereignty and which are ones that all would benefit from avoiding, the world would be a better place”; P.J. Ellingsworth, International Tax in the 21st Century: The Role of the OECD, 61 Tax Executive 4, p. 279 (2009): “In the area of international tax, OECD Tax is the most objective and competent global organization”. After the 2007/8 economic crisis, the OECD became even more influential due to the support of the Group of Twenty (G20).
Wouters & Meuwissen, supra n. 41, at pp. 222-227. See also A. Christians, Taxation in a Time of Crisis: Policy Leadership from the OECD to the G20 (2010), 5 Nw. J. L. & Soc. Poly. 1, p. 36, republished in R.S. Avi-Yonah ed., International Tax Law, vol. 2 (Edward Elgar 2016): “The respective institutional capacities and roles of the G20 and the OECD suggest that the latter has a far greater role to play in developing tax policy ideas and bringing them to consensus positions, while the role of the former is in effect to syndicate those positions to a larger audience”; R. Eccleston, The Dynamics of Global Economic Governance: The Financial Crisis, the OECD and the Politics of International Tax Cooperation p. 87 (Edward Elgar 2012), characterizing the relationship between the OECD and the G20 as symbiotic. Since then, it has expanded its agenda-setting role, to the point where today almost everything concerning international taxation policy is processed and filtered by its group of experts. In this sense, the OECD creates what some authors in international relations (IR) have termed an “epistemic community”.
D. Ring, International Tax Relations: Theory and Implications, 60 Tax L. Rev., pp. 110-112 (2007). See also Christians, supra n. 24, at p. 22: “These [OECD] tax policy groups form an intertwined epistemic community that holds an important and influential position in the law-making order”. For the concept of epistemic communities, see P.M. Haas ed., Knowledge, Power, and International Policy Coordination (University of South Carolina 1997); M.K.D. Cross, Rethinking Epistemic Communities Twenty Years Later, 39 Rev. Intl. Stud. 1 (2013).

Given that the OECD is accountable only to its members, who are mostly industrialized countries, it comes as no surprise that it is often described as a rich country’s club looking after the interests of the “Global North”. The self-interested nature of the OECD’s decision-making is confirmed by: (i) the ITR’s inbuilt distributive bias towards residence or capital-exporting countries; (ii) the resilience of outdated concepts and methods, such as the “permanent establishment” (PE) and separate entity taxation on the basis of the arm’s length standard (ALS) for transfer pricing (despite this being ineffective and exceedingly difficult for poor countries to administer);
The OECD is known for vehemently rejecting alternative methods to income allocation, such as formulary apportionment, which could favour developing countries. See, e.g., A. Christians, Why Is the OECD So Afraid of Formulary Apportionment?, Tax, Society & Culture (5 Feb. 2005), available at http://taxpol.blogspot.com.br/2015/02/why-is-oecd-so-afraid-of-formulary.html. and (iii) the difficulty of linking international tax with the development agenda.
D. Lesage, D. McNair & M. Vermeiren, From Monterrey to Doha: Taxation and Financing for Development, 28 Develop. Poly. Rev. 2, p. 157 (2010): “Notably, international institutions such as the UN, IMF, World Bank, and OECD, as well as academics, have remained relatively silent on this front, while governments have not mandated and resourced them to carry out more research”. Nevertheless, the ITR has been constantly presented as a natural product of a “remarkable consensus”,
This wording has been employed by experts and international institutions to describe tax reform discourse amid developing and transition countries. Stewart, supra n. 36, at p. 356. or sometimes as a “flawed miracle”.
R.S. Avi-Yonah, The Structure of International Taxation: A Proposal for Simplification, 74 Tex. L. Rev. 1301, p. 1303 (1996). Given that the majority of those affected by the ITR were not invited to participate in its initial formation and, to date, still remain excluded from decision-making processes, these descriptions both seem rather absurd.
See L.A. Sheppard, Twilight of the International Consensus: How Multinationals Squandered Their Tax Privileges, 44 Wash. U. J. L. & Pol’y 61, p. 62 (2014), short version The Twilight of the International Consensus, Tax Analysts (2013): “[The international consensus] is a century-old, mostly European gentlemen’s agreement to give residence countries tax jurisdiction over income earned by their residents in source countries”. See generally T. Dagan, International Tax Policy: Between Competition and Cooperation (Cambridge University 2018).

Realizing the incongruity, some have tried to redefine the ITR in accordance with a theory of legal pluralism developed by Terence Halliday and Gregory Shaffer.
See T.C. Halliday & G. Shaffer, Transnational Legal Orders, in Transnational Legal Orders (T.C. Halliday & G. Shaffer ed., Cambridge University 2015). Highlighting the presence of power asymmetries between nations, and the role of expert knowledge in shaping cross-border tax rules, Philipp Genschel and Thomas Rixen describe the ITR as a structure made up of not always compatible “transnational legal orders” (TLOs).
See P. Genschel & T. Rixen, Settling and Unsettling the Transnational Legal Order of International Taxation, in Transnational Legal Orders (T.C. Halliday & G. Shaffer ed., Cambridge University 2015). At their “solid institutional centre” is the OECD.
Id., at p. 155.

According to Genschel and Rixen, TLOs in international taxation have two main objectives: to ensure double taxation relief and to curb tax competition. The original TLO dealt almost exclusively with double tax avoidance (DTA), while a new TLO emerged in the 1990s to address tax competition. Genschel and Rixen also explain that these two TLOs are interconnected. The way the ITR was designed initially, in order to provide double tax relief, triggered the pervasive problem of tax competition. In short, this ITR is therefore no miracle and it is certainly flawed. This notwithstanding, and except for piecemeal amendments,
In the 1960s, the US government unilaterally introduced anti-avoidance measures – the so-called controlled foreign corporation (CFC) rules – and then pressured other countries to do the same, so that US corporations would not have to face a competitive disadvantage. Genschel & Rixen, supra n. 55, at p. 170. See also R.S. Avi-Yonah, Constructive Unilateralism: U.S. Leadership and International Taxation, 1 U. Mich. Pub. Law & Legal Theory, Research Paper n. 463 (University of Michigan 2015), available at https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2622868: “Initially, the adoption of Subpart F seemed to have put US-based multinationals at a competitive disadvantage, because no other country had such rules. But gradually this picture changed. [...] This is therefore a classic example of constructive unilateralism. The US led and others followed, and the end result is that most multinationals are subject to similar effective tax rates, with no competitive disadvantage or advantage”. the current “consensus” has remained almost unchanged because, despite its flaws, it still benefits OECD member countries.
Genschel & Rixen, supra n. 55, at p. 169: “Although these states may be suffering from tax competition with tax havens, they benefit from the Model Convention’s residence-based rules on passive income in relation to non-tax haven states. Because most states continue to be non-havens, and because many of these non-havens heavily import Western capital and technical know-how, Western states have a vested interest in preserving the TLO ... and closely related, Western states have an interest in preserving the OECD as the single and uncontested center of the TLO because doing so locks in their disproportionate influence over the contents of the TLO rules”.

For all these reasons, it may be more accurate to describe the current framework of international tax rules as a form of international fiscal imperialism.
See S.A. Rocha, International Fiscal Imperialism and the “Principle” of the Permanent Establishment, 68 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 2 (2014), Journals IBFD; Antón, supra n. 3. Countries have been pressured to conform to international standards fixed by the OECD.
The 1998 OECD’s initiative that attempted to incriminate what it deemed to be “harmful” tax practices is a case in point. See, e.g., K.B. Brown, Harmful Tax Competition: The OECD View, 32 Geo. Wash. J. Int’l L. & Econ. (1999); A. Townsend Jr., Global Schoolyard Bully: The Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development’s Coercive Efforts to Control Tax Competition, 25 Fordham Int’l L. J. 215 (2001); V.E. James, Twenty-First Century Pirates of the Caribbean: How the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development Robbed Fourteen CARICOM Countries of Their Tax and Economic Policy Sovereignty, 34 U. Miami Inter-American L. Rev. 1 (2002); K. Carlson, When Cows Have Wings: An Analysis of the OECD’s Tax Haven as It Relates to Globalization, Sovereignty and Privacy, 35 J. Marshall L. Rev. 163 (2002); R. Sanders, The Fight Against Fiscal Colonialism: The OECD and Small Jurisdictions, 365 The Round Table (2002); J.C. Sharman, Havens in a Storm: The Global Struggle for Tax Regulation (Cornell University 2006); S. Jogarajan & M. Stewart, Harmful Tax Competition: Defeat or Victory?, 22 Australian Tax Forum (2007); R.T. Kudrle, The OECD’s Harmful Tax Competition Initiative and the Tax Havens: From Bombshell to Damp Squib, 8 Global Econ. J. 1 (2008). Fully understanding how international tax relations are structured entails holding a critical view of current practices of global tax governance.
Global governance in general – but not global tax governance in particular – has already been extensively interrogated and problematized by critical legal scholars, following the leading work of Professor David Trubek of the University of Wisconsin Law School. See generally G. de Búrca, C. Kilpatrick & J. Scott eds., Critical Legal Perspectives on Global Governance: Liber Amicorum David M. Trubek (Hart 2014). It is only by doing so that one can grasp the real nature of the ITR, or tax TLOs, namely, international arrangements essentially based on relations of unequal power.
D. Kennedy, Law and the Political Economy of the World, in Critical Legal Perspectives on Global Governance: Liber Amicorum David M. Trubek p. 95 (G. de Búrca, C. Kilpatrick & J. Scott eds., Hart 2014), attributing to international legal scholarship the role of contributing to “situat[e] international law in the larger global political and economic system, interpreting its role as the glue linking leading and lagging ideas, regions, or economic sectors, or as the cloak hiding asymmetric dynamics of power in political economy of the world”; Koskenniemi, supra n. 21, at pp. 331: “The task for international lawyers is not to learn new managerial vocabularies but to use the language of international law to articulate the politics of critical universalism”. Pursuing such a critical project reveals that, in order to bring about a more just world order, the first step is to come to terms with these power dynamics, gaining more insight into who wields political authority in the global tax space.
D. Kennedy, The Mystery of Global Governance, in Ruling the World? Constitutionalism, International Law, and Global Governance p. 38 (J.L. Dunoff & J.P. Trachtman eds., Cambridge University 2009): “If we understood the machinery by which inequalities and hierarchies of influence and wealth and knowledge are reproduced, we might know how to make the world a better place”. What these dynamics indicate is that input from the majority of the world’s population is persistently excluded from impacting the processes of policy setting, thereby perpetuating historical patterns of economic domination and exploitation.
See Forst, supra n. 7, at pp. 256-257, arguing that “the discussion of principles of transnational justice is to start from an analysis of the present global context of injustice”, which “must be viewed as one of a complex system of power and domination with a variety of powerful actors, from international institutions to transnational corporations, local elites, and so forth”. More than that, it is only by “[e]xercising our critical muscles, [that] we can discourage being carried away by the dream of universal values”. D. Kennedy, Preface, in New Approaches to International Law: The European and the American Experience p. xi (J.M. Beneyto & D. Kennedy eds., Springer 2012).

section.title: Alternatives to Our “Club Model” of Global Tax Governance: The Problem of CO2
section.p:
During the past decades, a series of proposals for reforming the ITR has been advanced by prominent public figures, who have either called for the creation of a stand-alone ITO or for the strengthening of existing international cooperation forums. Among these have been:
Spencer, supra n. 46, at pp. 224-227. Vito Tanzi, former IMF economist; the late Kofi Annan, former UN Secretary-General; Ernesto Zedillo, former Mexican President; Frances Horner, former OECD staff; and the late tax law professor Michael McIntyre, former consultant to the UN Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters. More recently, some scholars in tax and other areas have re-examined these ideas in a new light, trying to provide philosophical and economic justifications for global tax reform.

section.title: The rise and fall of the ITO and the forgotten problem of power
section.p:
The first systematic proposal for the creation of an International Tax Organization (ITO) came in 1999 from Vito Tanzi, an Italian economist known for the so-called “Oliveira-Tanzi effect”. Following his previous work on the impact of the process of liberalization of markets and economic integration on national tax systems,
See V. Tanzi, Taxation in an Integrating World (Brookings Institution 1995), discussing the development of opportunities for tax avoidance and evasion that make it ever more difficult for states to maintain high levels of taxation, let alone progressivity. Tanzi ended this book with the following statement (see p. 141): “There is no world institution with the responsibility to establish desirable rules for taxation and with enough clout to induce countries to follow those rules. Perhaps the time has come to establish one”. See also V. Tanzi, Globalization, Technological Developments, and the Work of Fiscal Termites, 26 Brook. J. Intl. L. 4 (2001), outlining (at least) eight “fiscal termites” that can corrode the tax bases of nation-states: electronic commerce and transactions; use of electronic money; intra-company trade; offshore financial centres and tax haven; derivatives and hedge funds; inability to tax financial capital; growing foreign activities; and foreign shopping. Tanzi stated that negative externalities and spillovers were being created and/or aggravated as a result of deepening globalization. This scenario, he argued, called for a global solution. The world was in need of an ITO, not for collecting taxes, but to deal effectively with a series of tax-related technical problems.
See V. Tanzi, Is There a Need for a World Tax Organization?, in The Economics of Globalization: Policy Perspectives from Public Economics (A. Razin & E. Sadka eds., Cambridge University 1999); V. Tanzi, The Future of Fiscal Federalism and the Need for Global Government: A Reply to Roland Vaubel, 25 Euro. J. Polit. Econ. 1 (2008); V. Tanzi, Lakes, Oceans, and Taxes: Why the World Needs a World Tax Authority, in Global Tax Fairness (T. Pogge & K. Mehta eds., Oxford University 2016). These included: (i) locating global trends and problems in the area of tax policy, in the same way as the OECD’s CFA has done for its member countries; (ii) collecting fiscal statistical data and making them available to as many countries as possible, following the IMF’s and the OECD’s works; (iii) periodically releasing “World Tax Development Reports”; (iv) maintaining surveillance of tax developments, in the same way as the IMF monitors macroeconomic policy; (v) helping countries coordinate or even harmonize their tax systems, in the same way as the IMF provides technical assistance; (vi) producing global tax standards; (vii) serving as an international tax forum for policymakers and experts; and (viii) serving as an international tax arbitral tribunal for solving disputes between countries.

In 2000, Kofi Annan presented a report to the Preparatory Committee for the UN project on Financing for Development (FfD), recommending a careful in-depth study on the viability of an ITO.
United Nations, General Assembly, Report of the Secretary-General to the Preparatory Committee for the High-Level International Intergovernmental Event on Financing for Development, A/AC.257/12 (UN 2000) [hereinafter Annan Report]. This study was to be conducted along with the IMF and other IFIs. In Annan’s own words, an ITO “could provide a global forum for the discussion of and cooperation in tax matters, including the sharing of national taxation experiences; the development of definitions, standards and norms for tax policy, administration and related matters; the identification of national tax trends and problems; tax reporting; and the provision of technical assistance to national tax authorities, particularly those of developing and transition economy countries”.
Id., at pp. 51-52. He went further and appointed the economist Ernesto Zedillo as chair of the High-Level Panel on FfD. In its recommendations, the panel briefly mentioned the possibility of creating an ITO.
United Nations, General Assembly, Report of the High-Level Panel on Financing for Development, A/55/1000 (UN 2001) [hereinafter Zedillo Report]. Twice making express reference to Tanzi’s article,
Id., at pp. 28 n. 4 and 65 n. 24. the Zedillo Report listed six potential benefits of having an ITO: (1) to collect statistical data, pinpoint trends and problems, generate reports, offer technical assistance and produce global standards in the area of tax policy and administration; (2) to maintain surveillance of tax developments, in the same way as the IMF monitors macroeconomic policy; (3) to curb tax competition; (4) to develop arbitration procedures; (5) to take up the OECD’s project on information exchange in tax matters, developing multilateral mechanisms for curtailing tax evasion; and (6) to promote unitary taxation with formulary apportionment.
Id., at pp. 6-7, 28 and 65. For a defence of Zedillo’s proposal and an argument for why the United States should have supported the ITO, see W. Whisenhunt, To Zedillo or not to Zedillo: Why the World Needs an ITO, 16 Temp. Int’l Comp. L. J. 541 (2002).

One of the most important articles defending the creation of the ITO was written by Horner, after leaving the OECD, where she was responsible for the projects on tax competition and transfer pricing guidelines.
See F.M. Horner, Do We Need an International Tax Organization?, Tax Notes Intl. (2001). Her arguments are interesting because they give special emphasis to the need to include developing countries in global tax discourse. Criticizing the other proposals for not giving priority to the concerns of developing countries, and going against those who said an ITO would be redundant, Horner claimed that “a new global institution in taxation policy will make a significant, non-redundant contribution to global governance if – and only if – it gives a full and true voice to the fiscal concerns and needs of developing countries”.
Id., at p. 179. According to her proposal, five conditions were essential for an international tax arrangement to be valuable: (1) “no gag rules: all issues must be eligible for discussion at the forum”; (2) “fair share: attention should be given to profit allocation rules”; (3) “link to official development assistance: development issues should be relevant in formulating tax policy”; (4) “tax administration efficiency: developed countries should assist developing countries in improving tax administration”; and (5) “governance: developing countries should have a meaningful voice in any world tax”.
Id., at pp. 185-187.

A 2003 report by the UN Ad Hoc Group of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters, assisted by Professor McIntyre, also discussed the need to give developing and transitional countries a seat at the table.
United Nations, Secretariat, Ad Hoc Group of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters, Institutional Framework for International Tax Cooperation, ST/SG/AC.8/2003/L.6 (UN 2003) [hereinafter McIntyre Report]. The McIntyre Report called for a new institutional framework built upon the UN framework, but restructured “to serve the needs of developing and transitional countries for assistance and support on international tax matters”.
Id., at p. 14. The proposal sought to transform the existing UN Ad Hoc Group from an expert group to an intergovernmental commission with an independent budget, whose central goal would be “to give the developing and transitional countries an effective voice when issues of cooperation on tax matters are being decided by the international community”.
Id., at p. 15.

Despite all these efforts, the proposed ITO never took root. The main reason is political power: the OECD actively lobbied the G7 against the ITO.
V. Tanzi, Globalisation and Coordination of Fiscal Policies, Proceedings of a Conference Organized by the School of International Studies, University of Trento, Working Paper 03/2004 11 (University of Trento 2004). In addition, the OECD, joining forces with two Bretton Woods institutions (the IMF and World Bank), managed to find a substitute for an ITO, which they called the International Tax Dialogue (ITD). The ITD platform is neither an international institution nor a global forum for negotiation and cooperation.
Lesage, McNair & Vermeiren, supra n. 50, at p. 164. Instead, it is better described as a “networked governance”.
D. Lesage, Global Taxation Governance after the 2002 UN Monterrey Conference, 36 Oxford Develop. Stud. 3, p. 288 (2008). The ITD was created in April 2002 with the declared purpose of collecting and sharing tax information, and promoting technical debate on tax-related issues. Its creation reveals the desire of the three IOs, the OECD key among them, to retain control of the international tax agenda.
Curiously, the BEPS Project can be seen as the latest attempt of the OECD “to reclaim relevance” in international taxation. See Sheppard, supra n. 53, at p. 62.

To sum up, except maybe for Horner’s proposals, none of the aforementioned proposals were explicitly dedicated to providing a strong justification for an inclusive political forum that could empower developing countries. Nor was the need to put the global tax debate into a wider perspective – e.g. including developmental and other interests of poor nations – seriously considered. Most of the proposals were formulated on the assumption that the major fiscal problems facing countries today are technical, leaving the power problem in international tax relations unanswered. As Horner herself writes, “[f]or the developing countries of the world, taxation policy and the development agenda are inseparable” and “rich countries make a serious mistake when they act globally to address either one of these issues on a stand-alone basis”.
Horner, supra n. 73, at p. 179. Consequently, “[d]eveloping countries might judge the sincerity of any global tax cooperation effort by reference to how many of these key issues are open to discussion and how many are ‘off the table’”.
Id.

section.title: New proposals for international tax cooperation: Expertise and power, one more time
section.p: More recently, the idea of enhancing tax cooperation among countries has been reintroduced in academic circles. First, three political theorists, Thomas Rixen, Peter Dietsch and Douglas Bamford, have initiated a discussion on the principles of international tax justice that should be enforced by an adjudicating ITO, modelled on the WTO’s Dispute Settlement System (DSS). Second, three tax scholars, H. David Rosenbloom, Noam Noked and Mohamed S. Helal, have put forward a new proposal for international tax cooperation, one that is different from an ITO.

section.title:
Who actually needs an international tax court?
See ITO, op. cit.
section.p: The idea of an ITO has captivated not only economists, tax scholars and public authorities; lately, it has also caught the attention of academics interested in the topic of global tax justice.

The political economist Rixen was the first to address this issue. He began to make the case for an ITO in his work on the relationship between tax competition and the rise of inequality. In his early work on this subject, Rixen argued for an institutionalist view of tax competition, according to which the problem of under – or no taxation – should be seen as nothing more than an (unintended) by-product of the DTA regime set up in the 1920s and 1930s.
See T. Rixen, From Double Tax Avoidance to Tax Competition: Explaining the Institutional Trajectory of International Tax Governance, 18 Rev. Int’l Pol. Econ. 2 (2010). See also A.H. Rosenzweig, Why Are There Tax Havens?, 52 William & Mary L. Rev. 923, pp. 928-929, contending that tax havens are “a symptom of the unintentional incentives created and perpetuated by the modern international tax regime embodied in the laws of countries such as the United States”. Thereafter, Rixen used economic analysis and social contract theory to demonstrate that the phenomenon of tax competition – including tax evasion and avoidance – is fundamentally unjust, because it causes inequality between and across borders.
See T. Rixen, Tax Competition and Inequality: The Case for Global Tax Governance, 17 Glob. Governance (2011).

According to Rixen, tax competition undermines the integrity of tax jurisdictions and the state’s capacity to promote a particular conception of justice through redistributive taxation. As a possible solution to this global issue, he has advocated a system of global tax governance under an ITO. He proposes a supranational organization that would be entrusted with lawmaking and enforcement powers, sanctioning governments that engage in tax competition. Its main function would be to administer a regime of unitary taxation with formulary apportionment (UT+FA), but Rixen conceded that it could also serve as a forum for multilateral negotiations, so that countries could reach agreement on a common consolidated corporate tax base.

Rixen acknowledged that the only way to secure maximum inclusion would be for the ITO to operate under the institutional framework of the United Nations, but he considered this to be an idealistic scenario.
Id., at pp. 459 and 461: “the ITO should ideally be under the auspices of the United Nations”; “the successful implementation of UT+FA should be undertaken by an international tax organization that would ideally operate under the auspices of the UN”. Presumably, the need for an ITO would still be valid even if separate from the United Nations. Its priority would be to put an end to tax competition, even in the absence of a universal consensus on the appropriate allocation formula for the division of the global tax base.

Working subsequently with philosopher Peter Dietsch, Rixen sought to elaborate on the substantive principles of “global background justice”
They take this concept from Miriam Ronzoni, who expands globally John Rawls’s idea of “background justice”, i.e. a set of necessary conditions for justice. Compare M. Ronzoni, The Global Order: A Case of Background Injustice? A Practice-Dependent Account, 37 Philo. & Pub. Affairs. 3 (2009), with J. Rawls, Political Liberalism sec. 4, “The Importance of Background Justice” (2nd ed., Columbia University 2005). as a means to guide international taxation as a normative matter.
See Dietsch & Rixen, Tax Competition and Global Background Justice, supra n. 32; Dietsch & Rixen, Redistribution, Globalisation, and Multi-Level Governance, supra n. 32. Dietsch and Rixen begin by trying to demonstrate why some fiscal activities are problematic from a universal viewpoint, as though all the countries in the world could, or should, concur in rejecting tax competition. Relying again on empirical studies, Dietsch and Rixen reiterate that curbing harmful tax practices would be in the interest of all states, because of its effects on exacerbating social inequalities – especially among developing countries, which are seen by some as the biggest losers in tax competition.
