Developed countries, so the argument goes, lose out on their capacity to redistribute, but get to maintain their ability to raise revenue by adopting “tax cut cum base broadening” measures; as for developing countries, whose tax systems are unstable, losses would include both components. Notably, this argument in particular depends almost exclusively on a 2004 study that revealed a stronger decline in developing countries’ tax revenues. However, as the authors of that study warned in the end: “[t]he results reported here are preliminary. They may be contaminated by data inaccuracies or incompleteness of country coverage or reflect the dates chosen for the comparisons. The first lesson is that, even more than usual, more work is needed to confirm the story beginning to emerge”. M. Keen & A. Simone, Is Tax Competition Harming Developing Countries More Than Developed?, Tax Notes Int’l, p. 1325 (2004). It is worth noting that some authors have doubted that tax competition is that problematic. See, e.g. J. Roin, Competition and Evasion: Another Perspective on International Tax Competition, 89 Geo. L. J. 543 (2001); Y. Margalioth, Tax Competition, Foreign Direct Investments and Growth: Using the Tax System to Promote Developing Countries, 23 Va. Tax Rev. 161 (2003); M. Littlewood, Tax Competition: Harmful to Whom?, Mich. J. Int’l L. 411 (2004); J. Douglas Wilson & D.E. Wildasin, Capital Tax Competition: Bane or Boon, 88 J. Pub. Econ. (2004); A.P. Morriss & M. Lota, Cartelizing Taxes: Understanding the OECD’s Campaign against ‘Harmful Tax Competition’, 4 Colum. J. Tax L. 1 (2012); P. Penteado Neto, International Trade Subsidy Rules and Tax and Financial Export Incentives: From Limitations on Fiscal Sovereignty to Development-Inducing Mechanisms (AuthorHouse 2012); P. Genschel & L. Seelkopf, Winners and Losers of Tax Competition, in Global Tax Governance: What Is Wrong with It and How to Fix It (P. Dietsch & T. Rixen eds., ECPR 2016); Dagan, supra n. 53; L.V. Faulhaber, The Trouble with Tax Competition: From Practice to Theory, 71 Tax L. Rev. (2018). Perhaps the first question that would arise in this context is: what are these developing countries that (sometimes without even knowing it) are being harmed by engaging in tax competition?
Part of this argument relies, as noted by Diane Ring, on a problematic assumption that countries that engage in tax competition are necessarily in error, because they wrongly think “the benefits from competition outweigh forgone revenue, either because the benefits of that investment to the state were low or because business was likely to choose to invest in that state for other reasons”. D. Ring, Democracy, Sovereignty and Tax Competition: The Role of Tax Sovereignty in Shaping Tax Cooperation, 9 Fla. Tax Rev. 5, p. 566 (2009). But see H. Kelsen, Absolutism and Relativism in Philosophy and Politics, in What is Justice? Justice, Law, and Politics in the Mirror of Science: Collected Essays by Hans Kelsen (Lawbook Exchange 2013): “This belief [in absolute values] irresistibly – and has always – led to a situation in which the one who assumes to possess the secret to the absolute good claims to have the right to impose his opinion as well as his will upon the others who are in error. And to be in error is, according to this view, to be wrong, and hence punishable”. This is not to deny that tax competition should be a matter of concern.
See, e.g., R.S. Avi-Yonah, Globalization, Tax Competition, and the Fiscal Crisis of the Welfare State, 113 Harv. L. Rev. 7 (2000); R.S. Avi-Yonah, Globalization and Tax Competition: Implications for Developing Countries, 74 Cepal Rev./Law Quadrangle Notes (2001); R.S. Avi-Yonah, Bridging the North/South Divide: International Redistribution and Tax Competition, 26 Mich. J. Int’l L. 371 (2004); P. Dietsch, Catching Capital: The Ethics of Tax Competition (Oxford University 2015). Yet we should not overstate the importance of economic analysis, drawing conclusions without first consulting with the affected parties.
See Chang, supra n. 38: “If there is no one right answer in economics, then we cannot leave it to the experts alone”; W. Easterly, The Tyranny of Experts: Economists, Dictators, and the Forgotten Rights of the Poor (Basic Books 2013), disputing development experts’ and autocrats’ attempts to help poor countries and implying that they might do more harm than good; W. Easterly, The Tyranny of Experts: Foreign Aid versus Freedom for the World’s Poor, in The Economics of International Development: Foreign Aid versus Freedom of the World’s Poor (W. Easterly et al., IEA 2016): “if you thought that problems of poverty could be solved by expert solutions and by experts, there were already experts in 1938 that already knew the answers. It did not work then and it’s not working now”. The degree to which tax competition should be addressed and how this should be done are both issues that must be addressed democratically among all countries involved, and not solely by specialists in their ivory towers.
See J. Dewey, The Public and Its Problems: An Essay in Political Inquiry pp. 154-155 (M.L. Rogers ed., The Pennsylvania State University 2012): “No government by experts in which the masses do not have the chance to inform the experts as to their needs can be anything but an oligarchy managed in the interests of the few”; T. Piketty, Capital in the Twenty-First Century (A. Goldhammer trans., The Belknap Press of Harvard University 2014): “Democracy will never be supplanted by a republic of experts – and that is a very good thing”.

More importantly, does it even make sense, politically speaking, to divide a highly pluralistic world that is dominated by a multiplicity of interests
Our world is better described as a pluriversum, instead of a universum. See N. Krisch, Beyond Constitutionalism: The Pluralist Structure of Postnational Law (Oxford University 2010), developing a pluralist model of postnational governance; A.V. Teixeira, Teoria Pluriversalista do Direito Internacional (WMF Martins Fontes 2011), developing a pluriversalistic theory of international law; J.L. Cohen, Globalization and Sovereignty: Rethinking Legality, Legitimacy, and Constitutionalism pp. 21 and 269 (Cambridge University 2012): “We still inhabit a global pluri-verse of 193 sovereign states whose political cultures, organizational principles, conceptions of justice, and domestic legitimacy are diverse and at times in conflict with one another”; G. de Búrca, R.O. Keohane & C. Sabel, New Modes of Pluralist Global Governance, 45 N.Y.U. J Int’l L. & Pol. 723 (2013), arguing for a model of experimentalist governance, in order to enhance the understanding of global pluralism in law and politics. Compare Schmitt, supra n. 13, at p. 53 (“The political world is a pluriverse, not a universe”), with C. Mouffe, Agonistics: Thinking the World Politically (Verso 2013) (enquiring “about the implications of seeing the world as a pluri-verse”, and asserting that “[t]o take account of the political in its antagonistic dimension requires acknowledging that the world is a pluri-verse, not a universe”; “[t]he world is a pluri-verse, not a universe, and the Western model represents only one possible political form of life among others”; “[h]ere again I agree with Schmitt that the world is a pluri-verse, not a universe”). into these two extremely broad categories (developed and developing countries)?
It is possible to think of (at least) four groups of states, sorted out by similar positions concerning tax competition: (1) “OECD member states eager to limit tax competition”; (2) “OECD member states engaging in competitive behavior”; (3) “non-OECD states that feel ‘forced’ to engage in tax competition to secure significant, nonmobile business investment (e.g. manufacturing, production, etc.)”; (4) “non-OECD members engaged in tax competition over mobile financial and other activities”. Ring, supra n. 91, at pp. 563-567. Many other types of divisions are conceivable, as between rich and poor tax havens, high-tax and low-tax countries, countries that tax more consumption than income and vice versa, countries that have other sources of tax revenue, and so on.

Expanding their initial analysis, Dietsch and Rixen introduce the notion of fiscal self-determination (or tax sovereignty) as something at stake. Tax competition, they contend, undermines the de facto sovereignty of states by putting competitive pressure on domestic fiscal regimes. In order to counter this problem and ensure effective fiscal sovereignty, they provide two universal principles of tax justice. The first is the so-called principle of membership, which holds that “[n]atural and legal persons should be liable to pay tax in the state of which they are a member”.
Dietsch & Rixen, Tax Competition and Global Background Justice, supra n. 32, at p. 158. The second is that of fiscal constraint, a much more complex idea, requiring an evaluation of the legitimacy of each countries’ tax practices on a case-by-case basis. To accomplish the latter goal, they consider, and then reject, two possible strategies: an outcome-based constraint
Id., at p. 162: “A tax policy is legitimate if it does not produce a collectively suboptimal outcome. A collectively suboptimal outcome is here defined as one where the aggregate extent of fiscal self-determination of states is reduced”. and an intention-based constraint.
Id., at p. 162: “Rather than trying to delineate legitimate fiscal interdependence from illegitimate tax competition by appeal to outcomes, an alternative strategy is to focus on the intention that motivates the tax policy in question”. Since the first would leave little room for states to exercise their tax sovereignty and the second would overshoot the target by banning policies that supposedly benefit everyone (e.g. investments in infrastructure and human capital), they conclude that the most appropriate criterion would be a mixed constraint: “a tax policy is legitimate if it does not produce a collectively suboptimal outcome, that is, a negative impact on the aggregate extent of fiscal self-determination”.
Id., at p. 164. To illustrate, Table 1 summarizes what is prohibited and what is allowed under the mixed constraint:
Id., at p. 165.

p.emph: Table 1 – A mixed constraint on fiscal policy

section.p:
Hence, a state would only be forbidden from exercising its tax sovereignty in cases where it has been proven that its tax system was strategically designed to lure capital from another state, and if this leads to a bad overall outcome. To institutionalize these principles of international tax justice, Dietsch and Rixen call for the creation of an ITO. This would have to be an inclusive supranational institution that is capable of providing a level playing field for all states, so that they can negotiate the rules of the international tax game. To ensure the agreed rules are followed, it should also be powerful enough to enforce them. For that reason, they model their proposal after the Dispute Settlement Body (DSB) of the WTO, which allows for the imposition of economic sanctions on non-compliant countries.
One last thing that deserves attention is that, as Dietsch and Rixen make clear, their proposal would depend on the establishment of an effective system of multilateral automatic exchange of information. (See id. at 167.) They do not discuss, however, the costs associated with the implementation of such a system, especially for developing countries, many of which do not even have income taxation as their main source of revenue. See, e.g., P. Rosenblatt, General Anti-Avoidance Rules for Major Developing Countries (Wolters Kluwer 2015):
Generally, developed countries have broad-based taxes. Direct taxes, particularly personal income and social contributions, and less corporate income – are responsible for most revenue. Indirect taxes represent a smaller share of the tax collection, especially on domestic consumption rather than on international trade. Developing countries have a very different tax structure. Indirect taxes are their main source of revenue, for they are “easy-to-collect”, have fewer administrative costs and are less sophisticated. Import tariffs and excises – particularly resource taxes – still play an important role. Seigniorage or inflation tax, although not a proper tax, is often high. Income tax is a minor source of revenue given its narrow tax base, high collection costs and ease of evasion by the self-employed. The tax base consists mainly of taxpayers who are subject to withholding taxes on wages, mainly of the public sector, and large businesses that depend on the finance sector and comply with accounting requirements. This structure has a great distorting economic effect as opposed to modern taxes.
See also Genschel & Seelkopf, supra n. 31.

In adjudicating tax disputes, Dietsch and Rixen would have the ITO reviewing national tax laws through the application of not only legal reasoning, but also (and most controversially) economic reasoning, in order to decide whether a specific fiscal policy should be prohibited under the terms of the proposed regime. But as legal scholars and practitioners are well aware, the act of interpretation is also an act of creation.
This was already clear to Hans Kelsen almost a century ago, as well as to H.H.H. Kelsen, Introduction to the Problems of Legal Theory pp. 77 and 83 (B.L. Paulson & S.L. Paulson trans., Clarendon 1992): “Interpretation is an intellectual activity that accompanies the law-creating process as it moves from a higher level of the hierarchical structure to the lower level governed by this higher level ... even the judge creates law, even he is relatively independent in this capacity”; H. Kelsen, Pure Theory of Law (M. Knight trans., The Lawbook Exchange 2009); H. Kelsen, General Theory of Law and State p. 134 (A. Wedberg trans., Transaction 2009): “The judicial function is thus, like legislation, both creation and application of law”; H.L.A. Hart, The Concept of Law pp. 272-275 (3rd ed., Oxford University 2012); H.L.A. Hart, American Jurisprudence through English Eyes: The Nightmare and the Noble Dream, in Essays in Jurisprudence and Philosophy pp. 125-126 (Clarendon 1983), writing that “[t]he Nightmare is that this image of the judge, distinguishing him from the legislator, is an illusion, and the expectations which it excites are doomed to disappointment – on an extreme view, always, and on a moderate view, very frequently”, and that “what the courts were doing seems to the English lawyer, at first sight at any rate, particularly hard to justify in a democracy”. See also T.R. Bustamante, A Criação do Direito pela Jurisprudência: Notas sobre a Aplicação do Direito e a Epistemologia na Teoria Pura do Direito, in Contra o Absoluto: Perspectivas Críticas, Políticas e Filosóficas da Obra de Hans Kelsen p. 423 (A.S.M.C. Matos e A.B. Santos Neto eds., Juruá 2012): “Even theories that aspire to go beyond the limits of the descriptivism in the pure theory of law, like the theoretical developments of legal argumentation by Robert Alexy, Aulis Aarnio, Aleksander Peczenik, Manuel Atienza and, in a sense, Neil MacCormick, do not move away from Kelsen’s explanation of the dynamic moment of law and the process of application of legal norms” [author’s translation]. Among tax scholars, Misabel Derzi has written an entire book on the law-making power of judges and its consequences to the ideals of the rule of law. See M.A.M. Derzi, Modificações Jurisprudenciais do Direito Tributário: Proteção da Confiança, Boa-Fé Objetiva e Irretroatividade como Limitações Constitucionais ao Poder Judicial de Tributar (Noeses 2009). Partially because of these observations, the idea of judicial review and the democratic legitimacy of constitutional courts are constantly under attack. See, e.g., T. Bustamante, The Ongoing Search for Legitimacy: Can a ‘Pragmatic yet Principled’ Deliberative Model Justify the Authority of Constitutional Courts?, 78 Mod. L. Rev. 2 (2015). For an overview, see J. Waldron, The Core of the Case against Judicial Review, in Political Political Theory: Essays on Institutions p. 199 (Harvard University 2016), arguing that judicial review “is politically illegitimate, so far as democratic values are concerned: by privileging majority voting among a small number of unelected and unaccountable judges, it disenfranchises ordinary citizens and brushes aside cherished principles of representation and political equality in the ﬁnal resolution of issues about rights”. This is so not merely in the sense that legal decisions translate “law in the books” into “law in action”, but mainly because the meanings of legal texts are themselves a product of interpretation.
I. Venzke, How Interpretation Makes International Law: On Semantic Change and Normative Twists p. 4 (Oxford University 2012): “The meaning of norms is the product of legal practice. Practice itself has to bear the burden of completing the lawmaking process in concrete instances by interpreting the relevant texts. A norm’s meaning does not lie hidden in or behind the norm text itself but is a product of interpretation. The practice of interpretation then also accounts for the semantic change and legal developments. Interpretations may further provide new reference points for legal discourse and they structure the space for legal argument. The practice of interpretation shifts and generates legal normativity. If nothing else, this position fundamentally challenges the common narrative according to which international law owes its existence and normativity to the consent of its subjects”. This makes the job of justifying the decisions of a supranational group of judges even more difficult,
In the specific context of international human rights law, it would be easier to argue that judicial review by a supranational court accords with democratic principles. For an attempt, see A. Follesdal, Why International Human Rights Judicial Review might be Democratically Legitimate, 52 Scandinavian Stud. L. 103 (2007); A. Follesdal, Why the European Court of Human Rights might be Democratically Legitimate: A Modest Defense, 27 Nordisk Tidsskrift for Menneskerettigheter 289 (2009). And about the judicial protection of the rights of taxpayers, see R.G. Antón, The Fragmentation of Taxpayers’ Rights in International Dispute Resolution Settings: Healing Anxieties through Judicial Dialogue, 10 World Tax J. 1 (2018), Journals IBFD. especially given that, in practice, the ITO envisaged by Dietsch and Rixen would operate as a technical, purportedly apolitical, body, producing global norms through the application of economic theory.

Although Dietsch and Rixen may view their institutional solution as a forum for negotiations, this is not entirely the case because states would not be allowed to challenge the two basic principles of international tax justice. The decision-making procedures would go as far as to “defi[ne] the rules in line with the membership principle and the fiscal policy constraint”.
Dietsch & Rixen, Tax Competition and Global Background Justice, supra n. 32, at p. 167. To be fair, they do consider the objection of an anonymous referee that their ITO “is another non-majoritarian institution which is provided with substantial enforcement powers but lacks democratic accountability and legitimacy”.
Id., at p. 169. But they insist on a consequentialist response that does not really solve the issue raised;
Id., at pp. 169-170: “This is an important concern. Yet, we believe that the status quo, with its hollowing out of effective fiscal sovereignty through tax competition ... is even more problematic in this respect. Although it formally guarantees democratic accountability, de facto this is not the case. Conversely, while an ITO may reduce the scope of fiscal policy issues for which there is direct democratic accountability, it would make sure that the latter can actually work effectively in this restricted realm”. in the end, their proposal has to rely on a far-fetched comparative analysis with the status quo, which they take to be the worst-case scenario.

More than disregarding the possibility of disagreement among countries on this particular matter,
See Ring, supra n. 91, at p. 574: “arguments regarding tax competition are not confined to an efficiency framework but extend beyond to encompass ideas of international political structure and global society. Some states resist efforts to change harmful tax practices on the grounds that these practices are valuable from their sovereign perspective (even if potentially inefficient globally). Of course the states challenging tax competition consider certain competitive practices to be harmful from their sovereign perspective. Thus, there is a clash of sovereign positions, and an appeal to global efficiency provides no clear trump card”. Dietsch and Rixen’s argument ultimately rests on their own assessment of the (un)desirability of tax competition. There is no need, in their view, to consult with each and every state first, or to give states the opportunity to express what they believe to be the best solution (or even to define the problems). Instead, Dietsch and Rixen imply that they already know what international tax justice requires.
This attitude amounts to a form of philosophical absolutism, whose classical followers, “the great metaphysicians, were in favor of political absolutism and against democracy”. Kelsen, supra n. 91, opposing political relativism and democracy to political absolutism and autocracy. Accord R. Rorty, An Ethics for Today: Finding Common Ground between Philosophy and Religion pp. 11-12 (Columbia University 2011), citing Pope Benedict XVI, who once wrote that “[r]elativism appears to be the philosophical foundation of democracy”, a stance taken by philosophers John Stuart Mill, John Dewey, and Jürgen Habermas. Significantly, in the desire to spell out universal tax norms, Dietsch and Rixen overlook the likelihood that other countries’ cultures, histories, traditions, world views and relative socio-economic reputations, might provide completely different principles (or different interpretations of the same principles). This is a position criticized by Martti Koskenniemi:

longquote.p:
It follows that a person, group, or a State that does not share [those universal rational norms] is not only of another opinion (or preference) but has made a mistake about something that that person, group, or State should think rational for itself, too. Universalizability in theory leads automatically to expansion as practice. If my principle is valid because it is universal, then I not only may but perhaps must try to make others accept it as well. In any case, I can rest confident that I know what principles apply not only to me and my group but to any person or any group. If I engage in contacts with them, I need not face them as equals. I need not be open to their preferences because I already know that mine are universally valid, for me as well as for them, too. I may (or perhaps must) be kind towards and patient with them, but the object of my encounter can only be the transformation of the way they see the world, having them accept my principles, too (because they are not really “mine” but universally good).
M. Koskenniemi, The Gentle Civilizer of Nations: The Rise and Fall of International Law 1870-1960 p. 490 (Cambridge University 2004).

section.p:
It is not necessary to search far to find disagreement on the principles of international taxation. Bamford’s critical essay on Dietsch and Rixen provides one good example.
See Bamford, supra n. 32. Bamford challenges both of Dietsch and Rixen’s principles from a philosophical perspective and provides two alternatives: the principle of relationship and the principle of counter-incentive. With respect to the first, Bamford emphasizes that not only may legal persons be affiliated with more than one country, but also natural persons, and this leads him to conclude that a principle that focuses on relationships would be more appropriate. For him, multiple relationships should be measured on the basis of economic allegiance.
For individuals, Bamford considers possible criteria such as birth, parentage, citizenship, education, residence and location of earnings from labour and/or from investment. In the case of companies, since head of office and of incorporation can be easily manipulated, he agrees with Dietsch and Rixen that we should apply formulaic apportionment. This is not an original criterion; rather, it is one of the core principles of the current ITR, which was established in the 1920-30s.
Economic allegiance was a criterion developed by the famous “four economics professors” that the League of Nations asked to elaborate a technical report on how to assign taxing rights among countries. These experts all came from developed countries: Italy, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom and the United States. See generally H.D. Rosenbloom & S.L. Langbein, United States Tax Treaty Policy: An Overview, 19 Colum. J. Transnat’l L. 359 (1981); M.J. Graetz & M.M. O’Hear, The “Original Intent” of U.S. International Taxation, in Follow the Money: Essays on International Taxation (M.J. Graetz, Lillian Goldman Law Library 2016). For a critical analysis of their report, see A. Christians, BEPS and the Power to Tax, in Tax Sovereignty in the BEPS Era (S.A. Rocha & A. Christians eds., Wolters Kluwer 2017). As for the second principle, Bamford proposes that, instead of putting a constraint on states’ fiscal policies, a “rewarding” system for high-tax countries should be introduced that ensures they get a larger bite of the global tax pie.
It is unclear what Bamford really means by the word “reward”. If what he has in mind is that high-tax countries would be able to tax exactly what they were supposed to in the absence of tax competition, then we are not talking about a reward system, but about a harmonized system aimed at neutralizing tax incentives from other countries.

Bamford’s proposal is itself not without problems, being also subject to a range of criticisms. For instance, it can be argued that taking the world as it is, here and now, rewarding countries with higher taxes means rewarding rich countries. In a world with vast disparities of power and wealth across countries, eliminating tax competition without some form of compensation (like a global redistributive scheme or, less ambitiously, a better allocation of global tax revenues) would amount to an extremely regressive world tax regime,
See T. Dagan, Just Harmonization, 42 U.B.C. L. Rev. 331, p. 361 (2010): “If political justice and redistribution within states is what we strive for, then – assuming tax competition drives tax rates to the bottom – countries do, indeed, need to coordinate their tax policies in order to prevent this race. Such harmonization, however, might benefit some (probably developed) countries while harming other (probably developing) countries. If, rather, cosmopolitan justice is what we seek, then a mechanism of cooperation far more elaborate than simple harmonization is required, whereby countries not only coordinate their tax rates and policies but also design a system for administering payments to welfare recipients”. aggravating inequalities among countries (i.e. “international/intercountry inequality”).
A.W. Cappelen, National and International Distributive Justice in Bilateral Tax Treaties, 56 FinanzArchiv/Public Finance Analysis 3/4, pp. 424-425 (1999), observing that the “choice [of international tax principle] is, to some extent, a choice between national and international inequality”. For the distinction between international inequality (inequality among nations) and global inequality (inequality among individuals), see B. Milanovic, Worlds Apart: Measuring International and Global Inequality (Princeton University 2005). It should be noted that this critique could also be applied to Dietsch and Rixen.
The same problem was identified in Thomas Piketty’s recent proposal to institute a global tax on capital. (See Piketty, supra n. 94). M. Medeiros, Piketty e Nós, Revista Piauí (May 2014), available at http://piaui.folha.uol.com.br/materia/piketty-e-nos/:
[International] inequality, for example, is not considered in the book’s [Capital in the Twenty-First Century] debate. The French author does not give due attention to research that reveals inequality between countries, which is as important as inequality within countries. One way to understand this is to observe what happens with the Gini coefficient, an index commonly used to measure income inequality. The world’s Gini is higher than the Gini of any single country. Here, Piketty’s proposed solution to the problem of inequality fails. A country’s taxation retains taxes within it and therefore does nothing – at least directly – to reduce inequality between countries. By taxing the rich, the U.S. government would be taxing profits that have been obtained in other countries and brought to the United States. As a result, it would reduce inequality in their own country, but not in the world. [Author’s translation.]
Accord M. Stewart, Redistribution between Rich and Poor Countries, 72 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 4/5, p. 309 (2018), Journals IBFD: “simply ‘taxing’ such wealth (even if it were easy) is not going to be enough. We really do need to redistribute it, either through public goods or through social welfare systems. These proposals leave the redistributive task to governments of countries to achieve and do not address redistribution from rich to poor across countries”. Moreover, Bamford’s counter-incentive principle could end up creating an incentive for all countries to increase their taxes, replacing a race to the bottom in tax rates with a race to the top in tax rates. Either way, it is hard to see how the fiscal self-determination, or de facto tax sovereignty, of states would not be undermined, since countries would continue to take decisions based on external forces.

Be that as it may, Bamford’s proposal illustrates how it is possible to reasonably disagree on the basis of normative principles. Whatever the merits or demerits of his two principles, the point here is that if various experts cannot agree among themselves, it is not reasonable to expect all the world’s peoples to simply abide by any one proposal. To put in David Kennedy’s words, “[p]eople disagree about the most fundamental things” and “the challenges we face [will not] yield to technical expert consensus” because “they are political”.
Kennedy, supra n. 64, at p. xi. See also J. Waldron, Law and Disagreement p. 1 (Clarendon 1999): “There are many of us, and we disagree about justice. That is, we not only disagree about the existence of God and the meaning of life; we disagree also about what count as fair terms of co-operation among people who disagree about the existence of God and the meaning of life. We disagree about what we owe to each other in the way of tolerance, forbearance, respect, co-operation, and mutual aid. Liberals disagree with conservatives; socialists disagree with market economists; the party of freedom disagrees with the party of community and both disagree with the party of equality; feminists disagree with those who want the government to stand up for ‘family values’; last-ditch defenders of the welfare state disagree with triumphant opponents of taxation; and pragmatists and utilitarians disagree with those who think the task of law is to vindicate the claims of order, retribution, and desert”. Different minds will differ on what principles should regulate international tax relations and, as a consequence, how to allocate tax revenues across countries, sharing the fruits of economic globalization.

section.title: Will another international technical forum make a real difference?
section.p:
A new way of dealing with global tax issues has been recently suggested by Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal.
See Rosenbloom, Noked & Helal, supra n. 12. The authors begin by highlighting the shortcomings of the current international tax system, which is full of inconsistencies, inefficiencies and loopholes. Then, they proceed to evaluate a series of international forums for tax cooperation: the OECD, the G20, the United Nations, the European Union, the WTO, the IMF, the WB, as well as regional forums.
The Inter-American Center of Tax Administration (CIAT), the Commonwealth Association of Tax Administrators (CATA), the Pacific Association of Tax Administrators (PATA), the Intra-European Organization of Tax Administrations (IOTA), the General Assembly of the Meeting and Studies Center of Tax Administrations Directors (CREDAF), and the African Tax Administrations Forum (ATAF).

Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal demonstrate that none of these existing international institutions “provide an adequate platform for countries with similar interests to engage in a true collaborative effort”.
Rosenbloom, Noked & Helal, supra n. 12, at p. 58. One possible exception they consider is the Joint International Tax Shelter Information Centre (JITSIC), which is a forum that brings together a small number of countries concerned with tax avoidance and tax shelters.
Australia, Canada, China, Japan, South Korea, the United Kingdom and the United States. However, since the scope of the JITSIC is too limited for engaging with tax policymaking, they reject it as a viable model.
Rosenbloom, Noked & Helal, supra n. 12, at p. 75.

Their proposal consists in the creation of a novel international institution, but not exactly an ITO. Rather, they favour the establishment of an informal tax cooperation forum (TCF) modelled on what is known as a “like-minded group” (LMG). Stressing the importance of multilateralism, they argue informal forums could promote efforts with respect to: (i) multilateral tax treaties; (ii) formulary apportionment or other alternatives to transfer pricing; (iii) standardized anti-avoidance measures (e.g. CFC rules) and source and transfer pricing rules; (iv) tax arbitrage; (v) tax competition; (vi) harmonization of tax rates on capital income and of reporting rules; (vii) taxation of intellectual property; (viii) dispute resolution; and (ix) administrative cooperation.
Id., at pp. 82-83.

A serious difficulty with this proposal is that it lacks the necessary formality that could guarantee meaningful change. By suggesting that these TCFs could operate on an informal basis, Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal may contribute to perpetuating the same technocratic networked governance structures that have long been associated with problems such as “technocratic elitism”, “distortion of national political processes”, “unrepresentative input into global political processes”, “the ineradicability of power”, and so forth.
A.-M. Slaughter, A New World Order pp. 217-260 (Princeton University 2004). This unwarranted faith in informality seems to stem from a particular short-sighted view of IR, usually attributed to the neoliberal tradition. When such IR theorists, also known as liberal institutionalists, talk about international regimes, they confine their analysis to governments’ attempts to overcome market failures, such as the inconsistencies, inefficiencies and loopholes emphasized by Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal. But in doing so, they pay inadequate attention to the dynamics of power struggles. Drawing on the neorealism in IR (identified by authors like Kenneth Waltz and Stephen Krasner), Diane Ring contends that power can shape the international tax game, since powerful states can determine (i) who can join the game, (ii) what the rules of game are (e.g. who can make the first move), and even (iii) the order of preferences of weaker players (the so-called “payoff matrix”).
Ring, supra n. 25, at p. 101. This analysis accurately captures an observation made by Horner in her 2001 article: “[t]he OECD likes to claim that it develops the ‘rules of the game’, but unfortunately not all players are at the table”.
Horner, supra n. 73, at p. 182. Horner further noticed that “[i]f the OECD wants to be the facilitator of truly global rules of the game, it must find a way to put the developing world at the table with a real and meaningful voice”.
Id., at p. 179.

Likewise, Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal do not take into consideration recent debates in democratic theory, regarding political institutional practices that extend beyond the nation state. This is the focus of section 4.

section.title: International Tax Law between Legality and Legitimacy
section.p: This section builds on the ideas of political and democratic theorists, as they apply to the world order. The goal is to situate international taxation within the legitimation requirements of procedural fairness, deliberation and participation, with the aim of constructing political decision-making capabilities of dialogue and communication in and beyond the state. First, this section addresses a possible solution: the argument that legalizing the world of tax – converting “tax governance” into “tax law” – could domesticate power and control arbitrariness. It then proceeds to explore the politics of global tax governance and a possible democratically centred model for tax law and policy, as an alternative for either a global tax regulator or a global tax court.

section.title: From international tax soft law to international tax hard law: Why legalization is not enough
section.p:
One claim that has been made is that world politics should be subjected to legalization.
J.L. Dunoff & M.A. Pollack, International Law and International Relations: Introducing an Interdisciplinary Dialogue, in Interdisciplinary Perspectives on Law and International Relations: The State of the Art p. 3 (J.L. Dunoff & M.A. Pollack eds., Cambridge University 2013): “By the early 2000s, political scientists in turn ‘rediscovered’ international law, a development marked most clearly by the publication of a special issue of International Organization, the leading journal in the field, devoted to understanding the causes and consequences of the ‘legalization’ of international politics”. The articles in this special issue were republished in book form as J.L. Goldstein et al. eds., Legalization and World Politics (The MIT 2001). The idea is that if a system of international hard (tax) law could be created in place of the current system of international soft (tax) law, the legitimacy issue would resolve itself.
This is the motivation behind the OECD’s so-called “multilateral instrument” or MLI (Multilateral Convention to Implement Tax Treaty Related Measures to Prevent Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (7 June 2017), Treaties IBFD). See Christians, supra n. 4, at p. 1602: “the multilateral codification function may be an exercise in creping harmonization as well as a means to gradually ossify other forms of OECD soft law into hard law. [...] It is, finally, the multilateral agreement on tax that the original experts of the League of Nations had so hoped for and worked toward”. However, the legalization of global (tax) governance alone is far from sufficient. The absence of a broader democratic scheme that ensures inclusiveness, accountability, transparency and responsiveness threatens to consolidate the privileged position of powerful nations and private groups. In the presence of a new institutional superstructure, these global actors would focus on validating and reasserting their existing influence and expertise.
See S. Picciotto, Regulating Global Corporate Capitalism p. 468 (Cambridge University 2011).

Besides, “[d]epending on the governance structure of the international cooperation arrangement or world tax body, developing countries might find themselves working to an agenda that mainly helps countries that are already leaders in transnational business”.
Horner, supra n. 73, at p. 183. On the one hand, “[i]t would be naïve to see ‘networked governance’ separately from interests, ideas and power relations”.
Lesage, supra n. 81, at p. 288. On the other, “[i]t is clearly illusory to consider that law alone can provide adequate legitimacy for global governance”.
Picciotto, supra n. 132, at p. 468.

What emerges from the proposals by Dietsch and Rixen, as well as those by Bamford, is a legalistic, judge-centric system of international dispute settlement,
M.P. Cottrell & D.M. Trubek, Law as Problem Solving: Standards, Networks, Experimentation, and Deliberation in Global Space, 21 Transnat’l L. & Contemp. Probs. 359, p. 361 (2012): “legalist approaches to international law emphasize command and control regulation in which courts and similar bodies that apply sanctions for non-compliance lay down and enforce relatively specific rules that define allowable behavior”. For a classical concept of legalization, see K.W. Abbott et al., The Concept of Legalization, in Legalization and World Politics p. 34 (The MIT 2001): “Highly legalized institutions are those in which rules are obligatory on parties through links to the established rules and principles of international law, in which rules are precise (or can be made precise through the exercise of delegated authority), and in which authority to interpret and apply the rules has been delegated to third parties acting under the constraint of rules”. See also K.W. Abbott & D. Snidal, Law, Legalization, and Politics: An Agenda for the Next Generation of IL/IR Scholars, in Interdisciplinary Perspectives on Law and International Relations: The State of the Art p. 34 (J.L. Dunoff & M.A. Pollack eds., Cambridge University 2013), distinguishing between “law” and “legalization”: “‘[l]aw’ refers to the legal system and the body of legal rules, procedures, discourses, and institutions existing at a point in time; ‘legalization’ refers to the process of adding to, changing, or subtracting from the body of law and the legal system over time”. according to which “nations would then face governance by a group of unelected, multinational judges striking down domestic laws on the basis of economic theory”.
G.R. Shell, Trade Legalism and International Relations Theory: An Analysis of the World Trade Organization, 44 Duke L. J. 829, p. 906 (1995). Dietsch and Rixen argue that in order for states to regain their de facto fiscal power, these same states would have to accept limitations on their de jure fiscal power imposed by the rulings of an ITO. But such a regime “is likely to promote cooperation by clarifying treaty norms only if countries perceive the resulting interpretation of the norms to be legitimate” [emphasis added].
R.A. Green, Antilegalistic Approaches to Resolving Disputes between Governments: A Comparison of the International Tax and Trade Regime, 23 Yale J. Int’l L. 79, p. 133 (1998). As the 40-year-long tax controversy between the United States and the European Union over US export subsidizing through income tax exemptions demonstrates,
This long-lasting dispute was initiated under the old General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) system, by the time the European Union brought a case against the DISC regime enacted by US Congress. The drama continued after the establishment of the WTO, with respect to the Foreign Sales Corporation (FSC), Extraterritorial Income Exclusion (ETI), and Jobs Act regimes, all of which were considered in violation of WTO law. the trade-off between de jure and de facto sovereignty “may depend in part on satisfaction with the outcomes”.
D. Ring, What’s at Stake in the Sovereignty Debate: International Tax and the Nation-State, 49 Va. J. Int’l L. 155, p. 225 (2008). The reaction of US politicians, who harshly criticized these decisions for infringing on the sovereign power of the United States,
Id., at pp. 219-223. exposes the ongoing lack of legitimacy of the WTO model.
Brauner, supra n. 27, at p. 316. For an international tax law regime to properly function, it has to be accepted and internalized by all of the parties affected. This requires not another body of legal experts, but instead mechanisms for open communication, experimentation, reflexivity and political contestation.

section.title: What about the so-called “political”? The Repercussions of Smith vs. Jones
section.p:
The democratic deficit problem constantly surfaces when proposals focus exclusively on the outcomes and forget the processes of formation of political will. A comment made by an anonymous reviewer of this article illustrates this point. It was emphasized that, although biased towards OECD member countries (and perhaps even more towards multinational companies), the ITR should nevertheless be appreciated for having increased cross-border trade and investment in the post-World War II liberal order, bolstering peace and reducing conflict.
But see A.H. Rosenzweig, Integrating Tax and Development Policy, JOTWELL (2013): “Originally, the policy behind tax treaties was to lower tax barriers to cross-border trade – as barriers dropped, trade increased, making everyone better off. What this theory did not take into account, however, was that this only worked for countries between which trade would flow. That is, countries with roughly similar economies. But what about small, capital poor countries? What would they get in return for signing a tax treaty with a wealthy country? The emerging consensus is: nothing”. The reasoning begs the question by trying to justify means with ends, concentrating solely on some sort of “output legitimacy”. Yet the very problem to be addressed – reflected in the quotation at the beginning of this article (“Who decides what and how to tax, and for whose benefit?”) – is precisely the lack of “input legitimacy” that exposes the antidemocratic nature of the current international tax order.

This appears acute when the ITO envisaged by Dietsch, Rixen and Bamford is considered. Their proposal would effectively serve as an international court to resolve tax disputes on the basis of predefined principles. This approach disregards that the underlying problems of international taxation lie squarely on reaching a universal agreement on what norms should regulate international fiscal relations, given that any choice already presupposes a division of the global tax base.
M. Sato & R.M. Bird, International Aspects of the Taxation of Corporations and Shareholders, 22 IMF Staff Papers 384 (1975); Avi-Yonah, supra n. 52, p. 1303; Cappelen, supra n. 117; T. Dagan, The Costs of International Tax Cooperation, in The Welfare State, Globalization, and International Law (E. Benvenisti & G. Nolte eds., Springer 2004); A. Christians, How Nations Share, 87 Ind. L. J. 1407 (2012); I. Benshalom, How to Redistribute? A Critical Examination of Mechanisms to Promote Global Wealth Redistribution, 64 U. Toronto L. J. 317 (2014); A.H. Rosenzweig, Defining a Country’s “Fair Share” of Taxes, 42 Fla. St. U. L. Rev. 373 (2015); I. Grinberg, Does FATCA Teach Broader Lessons about International Tax Multilateralism?, in Global Tax Governance: What Is Wrong with It and How to Fix It p. 8 (P. Dietsch & T. Rixen eds., ECPR 2016). Dietsch, Rixen and Bamford do not even specify the framework of decision-making processes under which their ITO would operate at the supranational level. When put into practice, their well-intentioned idea of building a just tax order runs the risk of simply creating yet another bureaucratic global apparatus that will exert a kind of post-democratic political authority.
This problem is also at the root of the legitimacy crisis of the European Union. See generally Z. Bauman, Europe: An Unfinished Adventure (Polity 2004); J. Habermas, The Crisis of the European Union: A Response (C. Cronin trans., Polity 2012); U. Beck, German Europe (R. Livingstone trans., Polity 2013); A. Giddens, Turbulent and Mighty Continent: What Future for Europe? (Polity 2014); W. Streeck, Buying Time: The Delayed Crisis of Democratic Capitalism (P. Camiller trans., Verso 2014); C. Offe, Europe Entrapped (C. Cronin trans., Polity 2015). See also H. Brunkhorst, The Legitimation Crisis of the European Union, 13 Constellations 165 (2006); T. Pogge, Politics as Usual: What Lies Behind the Pro-Poor Rhetoric p. 183 et seq. (Polity 2010). Such a purportedly pragmatic method, therefore, fails to engage with critical questions about how and why the form of supranational decision-making they envisage would accord with democratic principles.

The flaw underlying all international tax coordination proposals to date may be traced to what Fraser calls the “Keynesian-Westphalian frame”, a phrase that “is meant to signal the national-territorial underpinnings of justice disputes in the heyday of the postwar democratic welfare state, roughly 1945 through the 1970s”.
Fraser, supra n. 13, at p. 11. For the embracement of Fraser’s theory of global justice by a TWAIL-er (Third World Approaches to International Law scholar), see B.S. Chimni, A Just World Under Law: A View from the South, 22 Am. U. Int’l L. Rev. 199 (2007). The problem with this framing is that it no longer holds for a globalizing world, where peoples’ lives are more and more impacted by decisions taken outside the territorial state. Now that countries’ economies are more interdependent, and unilateral redistributive policies in one country are much harder to achieve,
J. Habermas, The Postnational Constellation and the Future of Democracy, in The Postnational Constellation: Political Essays p. 79 (M. Pensky trans., The MIT 2001). See also T. Dagan, The Global Market for Tax and Legal Rules, 21 Flo. Tax Rev. 148 (2017). the very idea of fiscal self-determination might lose some of its appeal. Most importantly, as justice claims transcend the national level, political principles must take on a transnational form, in order to make democratic governance possible.

Surprisingly, even authors trained in political philosophy neglect this political dimension of justice.
As Fraser explains, this flaw is evident among liberals and communitarians, with some exceptions (e.g. Michael Walzer, Iris Marion Young, Amartya Sen and Seyla Benhabib). By contrast, deliberative democrats (Jürgen Habermas, Amy Gutmann and Dennis Thompson), agonistic democrats (William Connolly, Bonnie Honig, Chantal Mouffe and James Tully), and republicans (Quentin Skinner and Philip Pettit) have all sought to develop powerful theories of the political, but arguably without making an explicit connection with the idea of justice. According to Fraser, who admits she incurred the same mistake in the past, “an adequate theory of justice for our time must be three-dimensional”. For this reason, she argues for a theory of post-Westphalian democratic justice that fully integrates struggles against maldistribution (the economic dimension), misrecognition (the cultural dimension) and misrepresentation (the political dimension). Fraser, supra n. 13, at pp. 192-193, 194 n. 9, 200 and 206. Like many others before them, the theories of Dietsch and Rixen, and Bamford say nothing about the political.
Reworking Carl Schmitt’s classical conceptualization of the political, Chantal Mouffe defends the institutionalization of a form of antagonism between adversaries (“agonism”), in order to prevent that others are seen as enemies to be destroyed. She also criticizes rationalist models of democracy – or what she calls the post-political vision of politics – for denying the ineradicability of social conflicts and negativity. See, e.g., C. Mouffe, The Return of the Political (Verso 1993); C. Mouffe, On the Political (Routledge 2006). See also C. Mouffe, Carl Schmitt’s Warning on the Dangers of a Unipolar World, in The International Political Thought of Carl Schmitt: Terror, Liberal War and the Crisis of Global Order pp. 152-153 (L. Odysseos & F. Petito eds., Routledge 2007): “The central problem that our current unipolar world is facing is that it is impossible for antagonisms to find legitimate forms of expression. It is no wonder, then, that those antagonisms, when they emerge, take extreme forms, putting into question the very structure of the existing international order. It is, in my view, the lack of political channels for challenging the hegemony of the neo-liberal model of globalization which is at the origin of the proliferation of discourses and practices of radical negation of the established order. In order to create channels for the legitimate expression of dissent we need to envisage a pluralistic world order constructed around a certain number of great spaces and genuine cultural poles”. When it comes to justice, the dimension of the political is the one that “tells us who is included in, and who excluded from, the circle of those entitled to a just distribution and reciprocal recognition”, and that “tells us not only who can make claims for redistribution and recognition, but also how such claims are to be mooted and adjudicated”.
Fraser, supra n. 13, at p. 195. Interestingly, the political dimension of representation addresses one of the most important questions about global governance: “If we wanted to do something about poverty or the environment – if we wanted to complain or protest or simply participate – to whom should we address ourselves?”
Kennedy, supra n. 63, at p. 38. Resembling the widely recognized democratic principle in tax law of “no taxation without representation”,
Note how the process of creeping fiscal harmonization carried out at the European Union has been criticized as a form of “European taxation without European representation”. Both the efforts undertaken by European governments (i.e. by members of the executive branch) and the “negative fiscal harmonization” resulting from the judgments of the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) lack democratic legitimacy, because such intergovernmental and bureaucratic structures leave Member States and taxpayers underrepresented. J.C. Nabais, A Soberania Fiscal no Actual Quadro de Internacionalização, Integração e Globalização Económicas, in Por um Estado Fiscal Suportável vol. 1: Estudos de Direito Fiscal p. 199 (Almedina 2005); J.C. Nabais, A Soberania Fiscal no Actual Quadro da Integração Europeia, in Por um Estado Fiscal Suportável vol. 2: Estudos de Direito Fiscal p. 29 (Almedina 2008). Fraser proclaims: “no redistribution or recognition without representation”.
Fraser, supra n. 13, at p. 189.

By relegating the political to the margin, theorists neglect deep-seated conflicts of interest and power struggles that are so dominant in the international scene.
Dean, supra n. 32, at p. 938: “The study of international tax is the study of conflicts”; O.A. Batista Jr., Por Que a “Guerra Fiscal”? Os Desafios do Estado na Modernidade Líquida [Why the “Fiscal War”? The Challenges of the State in the Liquid Modernity], 102 RBEP 305 (2011), arguing that states are always going to “war”, if not militarily, through their economic and fiscal systems; A. Christians, Putting the Reign Back in Sovereign, 40 Pepp. L. Rev. (2013):
Every nation-state in the world is both a participant and a potential victim in a global game of tax competition that erodes and undermines comprehensive income taxation wherever it is attempted. Each nation must safeguard its tax base against the aggressive maneuvering of its taxpayers, aided in their quest by too-easily manipulated rules and the complicity of foreign intermediaries – be they public or private – that stand to gain by being a mercenary in this global game. At the same time, each nation benefits itself by embracing mercenary behavior against other nations in order to capture as much global capital as possible, even if such action deprives other nations of revenue. [...] One way of looking at the post-Westphalian world is as a society of nations, divided along territorial lines, which are in direct competition with each other for the world's resources but enlightened enough to work with each other to prevent total war. In such a world, nations bind themselves and one another to the mast in international agreements with the intention of preventing the unwanted scenario of total destruction, but then engage in everyday acts of sabotage against these binds in order to advance their own interests at the expense of others. In taxation, the tendency toward this “agree in form, defect in practice” behavior is manifested in the means by which states create and manipulate for their own benefit an evolving set of international standards and norms (sometimes but not always of their own making) around a global flow of information about economic resources and their owners.
Outside the tax field, see R. Gilpin, War and Change in World Politics 7 (Cambridge University 1981): “International relations continue to be a recurring struggle for wealth and power among independent actors in a state of anarchy”; J.J. Mearsheimer, The Tragedy of Great Power Politics (W. W. Norton & Company 2001):
States employ a variety of means – economic, diplomatic, and military – to shift the balance of power in their favor, even if doing so makes other states suspicious or even hostile. Because one state’s gain in power is another state’s loss, great powers tend to have a zero-sum mentality when dealing with each other. The trick, of course, is to be the winner in this competition and to dominate the other states in the system. Thus, the claim that states maximize relative power is tantamount to arguing that states are disposed to think offensively toward other states, even though their ultimate motive is simply to survive. In short, great powers have aggressive intentions.
José Luís Fiori, Preface to Global Power, 2 Pers. World Rev. 125, p. 134 (2010): “For us, however, the real starting point of the ‘modern world system’ is embedded in the ‘national Economy-States’ ‘invented’ by Europeans and that became ‘power and wealth-accumulating machines’, endowed with a bigger ‘expansive compulsion’ than that of the first power and capitals established in Europe during the ‘long thirteenth century’”; R.D. Blackwill & J.M. Harris, War by Other Means: Geoeconomics and Statecraft (The Belknap Press of Harvard University 2016), expanding on the concept of geoeconomics, as the use of economic tools to advance geopolitical and foreign policy goals. Under the conditions of multiplicity of interests and values, trying to develop top-down principles that heavily depend on universally accepted outcomes, and then applying them to the entire world – as Rixen, Dietsch and Bamford attempt to do – is, to say the least, a daunting task.
See H. Patomäki, Global Justice: A Democratic Perspective, in Globalization and the Global Politics of Justice (B.K. Gills ed., Routledge 2008), maintaining that, in the face of a variety of competing conceptions of justice at the global stage, the quest should turn to the democratization of global governance systems; A. Sen, The Idea of Justice p. 410 (The Belknap Press of Harvard University 2009), advocating that the recognition of a plurality of reasons for justice recommends a reach of global democracy, and that “[t]he challenge today is the strengthening of this already functioning participatory process, on which the pursuit of global justice will to a great extent depend”. See also J.S. Dryzek, The Deliberative Democrat’s Idea of Justice, 12 Eur. J. Polit. Theory 329 (2013), reviewing Sen’s book, and concluding that “[d]emocracy does not guarantee justice, but in a world of plural justice claims democracy is necessary to the pursuit of justice”; L. Valentini, Justice, Disagreement and Democracy, 1 B. J. Pol. S. (2012), claiming that, in the presence of “thick reasonable disagreement”, democracy has intrinsic value, as a necessary demand of justice. Once it is taken into account that the world is pluralist and divided, it becomes clear how troubling it is to search for universalistic, all-encompassing solutions.
D. Kennedy, One, Two, Three, Many Legal Orders: Legal Pluralism and the Cosmopolitan Dream 31 N.Y.U. Rev L & Soc. Change 641, p. 658 (2007):
Our global political world remains decentralized and horizontal. There is no one “international community”. The phrase refers to the particular elite who are the audience for the global media. We must recognize the idea that they share a “consensus” view of global political or ethical matters – or that their views condense the attitudes of humanity – as a fantasy. It may often be a desirable fantasy, and we may often want to encourage it, but is a fantasy. And it can be a dangerous fantasy. It can encourage us to think there is, in fact, an “international community” ready to back up pronouncements made in its name. It can encourage political elites to start projects and launch interventions, for which there will be no follow-up. It can suggest that those who disagree with these elites – and many do – are somehow outside the circuit of “civilization”. It can lead us to imagine that we know what justice is, always and everywhere. But, of course, we do not. Justice is not like that. It needs to be made anew in each time and in each place.
The result is a major challenge to proposals such as those put forward by Dietsch, Rixen and Bamford.

In the first place, these theories are vulnerable to the claim that they do not accommodate deep diversity among countries, including the likelihood of disagreement on whether the status quo is better or worse than in a world free of tax competition. Some developing countries could readily complain, for example, that this new world order would make it extremely hard for them to attract investments. Since tax incentives would be off the table, and assuming that these nations might not have a business-friendly environment due to poor infrastructure, they might be left with no alternative other than try to make up for their lack of economic advantage by using arguably more harmful regulatory competitive strategies, such as relaxing (even more) labour and environmental legislations.

Countries disagree on what global solutions are actually needed and how they should be implemented, as in the obvious case of what should be the appropriate criteria for dividing the global tax base. In other words, Dietsch, Rixen and Bamford seem to disregard what John Rawls, in his mature work, came to dub “the fact of pluralism” – the diversity of comprehensive doctrines
This is one of the main reasons that led him to reformulate his theory of justice, so that it could make room for reasonable disagreement. Rawls, supra n. 88: “The fact of a plurality of reasonable but incompatible comprehensive doctrines – the fact of reasonable pluralism – shows that, as used in Theory the idea of a well-ordered society of justice as fairness is unrealistic”. – or what Jürgen Habermas prefers to call “competing worldviews”,
J. Habermas, Between Facts and Norms: Contributions to a Discourse Theory of Law and Democracy pp. 60-61 (W. Rehg trans., 1996). which are much more intensified and persistent on the global stage. Indeed, being confronted with the pluralist character of today’s societies, these two renowned philosophers felt they had no other choice but to redirect their respective theories towards what came to be known as a “procedural turn” in political theory.
See J. Rawls, The Idea of Public Reason Revisited, in The Law of Peoples: with “The Idea of Public Reason Revisited” (Harvard University 1999); J. Habermas, Paradigms of Law, in Habermas on Law and Democracy: Critical Exchanges (M. Rosenfeld & A. Arato eds., University of California 1998). On the Habermas-Rawls political dispute on how to address de facto pluralism of world views within the boundaries of the democratic constitutional state, see J.G. Finlayson & F. Freyenhagen eds., Habermas and Rawls: Disputing the Political (Routledge 2011). Moreover, in the case of Rawls, coming to realize that some of the assumptions he had made in his acclaimed theory of justice as fairness were implausible eventually led him to a “political turn”.
For the reasons why Rawls had to make this turn, see P. Weithman, Why Political Liberalism? On John Rawls’s Political Turn (Oxford University 2010).

In the second place, and probably most troubling for Dietsch, Rixen and Bamford, is the fact that their theories invite the heavy criticism that they perpetuate imperialistic forms of domination, oppression and injustice – which has been referred to as “informal (or postcolonial) imperialism”.
J. Tully, Public Philosophy in a New Key vol. 2: Imperialism and Civic Freedom pp. 134-135 (Cambridge University 2008):
Informal imperialism consists in, firstly, imposing a structure of domestic public law and political institutions, or “structurally adjusting” an existing constitutional order, that opens the resources, labour and markets of the imperialised country to free trade dominated by the great powers; and, secondly, subjecting this legal and political order in turn to regimes of public and private international laws, again constructed and dominated by the great powers.
In his critical reconstruction of how international law has been historically used to “civilize” the world, Koskenniemi refers to two forms of imperialism, rational and cynical, concluding that: “[r]ational imperialism turned out to be a façade for cynical imperialism”. Koskenniemi, supra n. 111, at pp. 491-492 and 500. See also A. Anghie, Imperialism, Sovereignty and the Making of International Law (Cambridge University 2004), providing an alternative historical theory of international law and sovereignty based on the idea of “civilizing missions”. Weak countries would see a new global project to curb tax competition as a reiteration of the OECD’s initiative on “harmful” tax practices: an attempt to deprive them of one of their few sources of power, leaving them at the mercy of more powerful states. Once again, citing Horner:

longquote.p:
Without a meaningful voice in such a forum, without basic conditions [...], developing countries may find that a global tax arrangement holds little interest and poses some dangers.
Horner, supra n. 73, at p. 187.

section.p:
A realistic analysis must take seriously, and engage with, the problem of international struggles for power and wealth. Such an analysis would show that merely ending tax competition is not a satisfactory solution to global challenges, even if any such end could be achieved in practice, a doubtful proposition in any case.
Focusing on formal legal change without looking at how power and influence shape the way law is implemented in practice is an approach that is almost certainly doomed to fail. In Brazil, for example, state tax subsidies are constitutionally prohibited, unless there is an agreement among all 27 federal units. But that has not been able to stop states from engaging in harmful tax competition via confidential agreements among taxpayers and government officials. As long as governments can issue private rulings to specific parties, hiding tax breaks from public view, a “law on the books” approach will never be able to unearth them. At the international level, the Lux Leaks case is a clear example of how, in the absence of effective accountability, transparency and democratic control, tax competition will never come to an end; it will only shift places. See A. Christians, Lux Leaks: Revealing the Law, One Plain Brown Envelope at a Time, Intl. Tax Notes (2014). Unless an articulated “international tax consensus” is the result of some sort of political-democratic method that includes subjects of the principles of fiscal justice – all states and their citizens – as co-authors,
I.M. Young, Inclusion and Democracy pp. 5-6 (Oxford University 2000): “The normative legitimacy of a democratic decision depends on the degree to which those affected by it have been included in the decision-making processes and have had the opportunity to influence the outcomes”. it can be nothing more than an instrument of control imposed by the most powerful players – a “globalization from above” or “hegemonic globalization”.
On these concepts, see R.A. Falk, Predatory Globalization: A Critique ch. 8 (Polity 1999): “Resisting ‘Globalization-from-Above’ through ‘Globalization-from-Below’”; P. Evans, Fighting Marginalization with Transnational Networks: Counter-Hegemonic Globalization, 29 Contemp. Socio. 230 (2001), calling for a counterhegemonic globalization movement; P. Evans, Counterhegemonic Globalization: Transnational Social Movements in the Contemporary Global Political Economy, in The Handbook of Political Sociology: States, Civil Societies, and Globalization (T. Janoski et al. eds., Cambridge University 2005), analysing countermovements in contemporary world politics; B. Rajagopal, International Law from Below: Development, Social Movements and Third World Resistance pp. 295-296 (Cambridge University 2003), defending an international legal theory based on “histories from below”, in order to “transform international law from an international law of domination to one of resistance in the aid of marginalized communities and peoples”; H.W. Afonso & J.Q. de Magalhães, Para Contar as Outras Estórias: Direito Internacional e Resistência Contra-Hegemônica no Terceiro Mundo [Telling Other Stories: International Law and Counter-Hegemonic Resistance in the Third World], 29 Rev. Fac. Dir. Sul de Minas 155 (2013); G.W. Anderson, Societal Constitutionalism, Social Movements, and Constitutionalism from Below, 20 Ind. J. Global Legal Stud. 881 (2013), incorporating transnational social movements within theories of constitutionalism beyond the state. See also P. Bond, Top Down or Bottom Up? A Reply, in Debating Globalization (Held et al., Polity 2005), criticizing David Held’s cosmopolitan project for not being radical enough; B. Barber, Global Governance from Below, in Debating Globalization p. 103 (Held et al., Polity 2005): “The challenge today ... is to create the foundations for global governance from below even as governments and international organizations face the challenge of giving teeth to their common covenants”. See generally B.S. Santos & C.A. Rodríguez-Garavito eds., Law and Globalization from Below: Towards a Cosmopolitan Legality (Cambridge University 2005).

Instead of yet again searching for a priori, ostensibly apolitical and rational principles of international taxation and then trying to construct an overarching coercive legal structure to achieve them, one should start from the bottom-up, thinking about how to include all those affected by the collective decision-making. Another intellectual strategy emanating from the Global North, no matter how elegant, therefore does not resolve the problem of the continuous exclusion of historically marginalized countries from initial regime formation. A legitimate ITR requires the improvement of discursive practices of decision-making at all levels of the globe (from the local to the global) – a political project that must include institutional choices of who should tax, where and what.
This question was posed by Richard Musgrave in a 1983 piece while dealing with interjurisdictional tax rules between subunits within a federation or confederation. See R.A. Musgrave, Public Finance in a Democratic Society vol. 3: The Foundations of Taxation and Expenditure (2000).

To achieve this kind of inclusion requires directing attention towards advancing mechanisms of participation and dialogue beyond the nation state, pushing further on for a comprehensive democratization of global tax governance. It is crucial to embrace basic legitimacy principles, such as the ideas of “global deliberative equality”,
Slaughter, supra n. 126, at pp. 245-247. and “participatory parity”,
This norm was introduced for the first time by Fraser, in her critique of Habermas’ conception of the public sphere. There, she asserted that, although full parity of participation within a polity is not always possible, we should still try to come as close as possible to this aspirational ideal by asking ourselves what institutional arrangements could narrow the gap between dominant and subordinate groups. N. Fraser, Justice Interruptus: Critical Reflections on the “Postsocialist” Condition pp. 80-81 (Routledge 1997). At the international level, this notion is recast to focus on “the patterning of the broader space within which bounded polities are embedded and, so, to the question of who is included, and who excluded, from the circle of those entitled to participate within them”, and to address situations “when the partitioning of political spaces blocks some who are poor or despised from challenging the forces that oppress them”. N. Fraser, K. Nash & V. Bell, The Politics of Framing: An Interview with Nancy Fraser by Kate Nash and Vikki Bell, in Scales of Justice: Reimagining Political Space in a Globalizing World pp. 142 and 147 (N. Fraser, Columbia University 2009). in view of creating more democratic and egalitarian global institutions. Only when states and their citizens acquire equally effective voices, having the opportunity to defend their specific fiscal interests and needs on an equal footing, while taking due account of (and also challenging) the perspectives of others, can the process of deciding global fiscal matters, and producing cosmopolitan tax norms, be deemed politically legitimate.
In a sense, this reasoning follows what Seyla Benhabib calls “democratic iterations”, i.e. a jurisgenerative politics of interpretation, argumentation, contestation, revision, resignification and rejection that should take place at various levels of the globe. As a practical matter, such an approach seeks to “reconcile cosmopolitanism with the unique legal, historical, and cultural traditions and memories of a people”. S. Benhabib, Democratic Iterations: The Local, the National, and the Global, in Another Cosmopolitanism p. 70 (R. Post ed., Oxford University 2006). It is also inspired by Jürgen Habermas’s democratic principle, which says: “only those statutes may claim legitimacy than can meet with the assent (Zustimmung) of all citizens in a discursive process of legislation that in turn has been legally constituted”. See Habermas, supra n. 157, at p. 110. Applying a Habermasian perspective to international tax law, Cees Peters concludes: “[t]here is no fixed idea about ‘fairness’ in international taxation and it is up to us mortals to define what is acceptable and what is not. [...] This should be a continuous and never-ending dialogue that is based on the recognition that taxpayers and states (and other players) are part of the international society as a whole. Only in this way it is possible to guarantee its legitimacy in the longer run”. Peters, supra n. 3, at p. 391.

section.title: Beyond technocracy and hegemony: For a pluralist and multipolar international tax order
section.p: Given the implausibility of the models proposed so far, and concluding that all the above-mentioned solutions are, in some way or another, at odds with the proceeds of political influence necessary to validate supranational norms and institutions, it is time to return to the question posed by Tanzi 19 years ago: is there really a need for an ITO?

The prospects of building an effective supranational tax institution with universal membership, meaning one that is really able to guarantee full inclusiveness and broad participation to all countries at once, do not seem very bright. An ITO would, at best, be part of the same international framework under the United Nations, an organization that has repeatedly failed to take action on behalf of poor countries, as in the case of promoting a more equitable ITR. Therefore, a comprehensive reform would be a vital first step.
But see Mouffe, supra n. 149, at p. 100: “as Danilo Zolo has convincingly argued, given the enormous disparity of power among its members, it is completely unrealistic to believe in the possibility of reforming the United Nations in order simultaneously to strengthen them and to make them more democratic”; M. Koskenniemi, Book Review: “Globalization and Sovereignty: Rethinking Legality, Legitimacy and Constitutionalism”, by Jean L. Cohen, 11 Int’l J. Const. L. 818, p. 821 (2013), stating that “[t]he time of UN reform certainly is [past], and perhaps the same applies to the use of the European Union as a ‘model’ for something”. This is not impossible, but even so, creating another techno-bureaucratic supranational body with enforcement powers will not resolve the political legitimacy deficit of global tax governance; on the contrary, it might aggravate it. Marginalized countries do not need another technical forum to make progress towards a more just ITR, and they should not have to wait for a major UN reform.
Lesage, supra n. 81, at p. 289: “the existence of an ITO is not a guarantee for developing countries to bring about a ‘new taxation order’; they will need counterparts that share their agenda. Otherwise, the ITO could influence the debate only in its function as an alternative epistemic community and at best generate change at a very low pace. Therefore, developing countries interested in an alternative tax agenda should not concentrate too much on the formal universal level”. To advance their cause, these countries need better opportunities for political articulation at different levels and in different regions, even outside the UN scope.
Id., at p. 290. This could contribute to the emergence of other epistemic communities “that counter[ ] the marginalization of alternative views on national and international taxation”.
Id., at p. 293.

To some extent, the LMG approach elected by Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal seems more appealing than all the competing proposals examined herein. Instead of ignoring the different fiscal interests at play – i.e. the heterogeneity of the tax world – the LMG approach embraces this plurality. However, giving in too quickly to the idea of informal regimes, the exponents do not address the issue of power and the political and distributional dimensions of international conflicts over taxation. As such, their model would only account for technical forums for the exchange of expertise among those countries that already have it.

No real space for political resistance, deliberation or empowerment is encouraged within Rosenbloom, Noked and Helal’s proposal. Yet this is precisely what is needed: to pursue the establishment of strong political multilateral forums at both regional and global levels, so as to foster democratic contestation, deliberation and power sharing.
Such coalitions are not that uncommon, even in the area of international taxation, as exemplified by the International Trade and Investment Organization (ITIO): “a somewhat misleading name for a forum of OFCs in the Caribbean and the Pacific, created in 2001 to defend their interests in reaction to the OECD initiative on harmful tax competition”. Lesage, supra n. 81, at p. 292. A more recent example of institutions based upon a coalition of developing countries is the BRICs. For an initial evaluation of its role in shaping international tax, see Y. Brauner & P. Pistone eds., BRICS and the Emergence of International Tax Coordination (IBFD 2015). Contrary to celebrating the tendency observed among BRICs countries of convergence to OECD tax standards, non-members should be encouraged to form change-seeking coalitions that can, in turn, challenge OECD supremacy and break the status quo.
Dries Lesage has pointed out to me that global civil society actors might play an important role in this regard. For important surveys, see A. Christians, Tax Activist and the Global Movement for Development through Transparency, in Tax, Law and Development (Y. Brauner & M. Stewart eds., Edward Elgar 2013); Tax Justice Network ed., The Greatest Invention: Tax and the Campaign for a Just Society (Commonwealth 2015).
