But these activist movements should be careful enough not to lose sight of how rich nation states and the global legal and political institutions they help create are ultimately “the ones to blame” for the lack of legitimacy and the unfairness of international tax. In a way, they should avoid promoting a strategy criticized by Chantal Mouffe for resulting in what she calls “withdrawal from” existing institutional structures, as opposed to “engagement with”. See Mouffe, supra n. 95. This has not gone unnoticed in the tax scholarship. As Allison Christians stresses, the media and many individuals and organizations protesting against global tax injustices have focused too much on tax cheats and too little on the law. They have invoked morality and pushed for soft law mechanisms, conflating evasion and avoidance, instead of demanding legal and political change. See A. Christians, Avoidance, Evasion, and Taxpayer Morality, 44 Wash. U.J.L. & Pol’y 39 (2014). Some developing countries, such as Bangladesh, China, India and Singapore, have already begun to question the way international tax norms have been drafted in the interests of developed countries.
See their respective Responses to the UN Subcommittee on BEPS Issues for Developing Countries, available at http://www.un.org/esa/ffd/tax/Beps/CommentsIndia_BEPS.pdf. The strategy, however, was badly received by Robert Stack, a US Treasury Deputy Assistant Secretary for International Tax Affairs, who “expressed a concern that countries such as India and China are seeking to use the OECD’s BEPS project to ‘recalibrate the global paradigm’ on source and residence-state taxation that has been in place since the 1920s’”.
J. Fuller & D. Forst, The U.S. Position on BEPS, 24 Intl. Tax R. 59 (2013). Such recalibration is indeed what these countries should aim for. In publicly expressing their dissent with the BEPS initiative, such nations have become the harshest critics of the OECD’s tax work so far, coming from the “Global South”. Though not yet “global norm-makers”, they are visibly transforming themselves into active “global norm-shakers”.
See, e.g., J. Li, China and BEPS: From Norm-Taker to Norm-Shaker, 69 Bull. Intl. Taxn. 6/7 (2015), Journals IBFD.

That is the way forward. Developing countries should target the pluralization of hegemonies in the postnational tax space through counterhegemonic projects and alternative epistemic communities.
See D. Kennedy, Three Globalizations of Law and Legal Thought: 1850-2000, in The New Law and Economic Development: A Critical Appraisal p. 24 (D.M. Trubek & A.Santos eds., Cambridge University 2006): “progressive elites of the periphery can and should devise national progressive strategies, rather than accept the prescription of the center”. Given that no political change is likely to come from developed countries, marginalized nations need to find other avenues for exercising resistance and demanding global change. For multilateralism to be more than mere wishful thinking, the aim must ultimately be for the creation of a multipolar world order, where power is more equally distributed between regional poles, and thus exerted in a more balanced manner.
As Mouffe argues, “[w]e hear a lot about the necessity of an effective ‘multilateralism’. But multilateralism in an unipolar world will always be an illusion. As long as a single hegemonic power exists, it will always be the one that decides if it will take into consideration the opinion of the other nations or act alone. A real multilateralism requires the existence of a plurality of centres of decision and some sort of equilibrium – even if it is only a relative one – among various power”. Mouffe, supra n. 149, at p. 116. Accord Teixeira, supra n. 95, at pp. 271-292. For this reason, a political project that intends to bring about a legitimate world order in tax – or any other field, for that matter – should be modelled on a “demoi-cracy”, an idea primarily developed for the EU context.
See K. Nicolaïdis, “We, the Peoples of Europe...”, 83 Foreign Aff. 97, p. 101 (2004): “The EU is neither a union of democracies nor a union as democracy; it is a union of states and of peoples – a ‘demoicracy’ – in the making”; S. Besson, Deliberative Demoi-cracy in the European Union: Towards the Deterritorialization of Democracy, in Deliberative Democracy and its Discontents p. 206 (S. Besson & J.L. Martí eds., Ashgate 2006): “The idea of demoi-cracy has now become an inseparable part of recent proposals attempting to remedy the democratic deficit in the European Union. It emphasizes the deliberation among many European demoi rather than either one European demos or many national demoi”. In a situation involving multistate governance, democratic principles require the possibility of deliberation among many demoi.
For an application of a similar reasoning, see J. Bohman, Democracy across Borders: From Dêmos to Dêmoi (The MIT 2007); F. Cheneval, The Government of the Peoples: On the Idea and Principles of Multilateral Democracy (Palgrave Macmillan 2011).

The institutionalization of such a model at the global level requires the development of three dimensions, insofar as “global democracy should be conceived of as pluralistic, deterritorialised and deliberative, i.e. in a nutshell as deliberative demoi-cracy”.
S. Besson, Institutionalizing Global Demoi-cracy, in Legitimacy, Justice and Public International Law pp. 59-60 (L.H. Meyer ed., Cambridge University 2009). To be sure, the goal is to build a “multilayered, multi-actor, multi-stratified system”,
A.V. Teixeira, Direito Público Transnacional: Por uma Compreensão Sistêmica das Esferas Transnacionais de Regulação Jurídica [Transnational Public Law: For a Systemic Interpretation of the Transnational Spheres of Legal Regulation], 19 Revista Novos Estudos Jurídicos 400, p. 424 (2014). but one that is “on the mode of a ‘demoi-cracy’ composed of a multiplicity of diverse demoi providing different spaces for ‘the exercise of democracy’”.
Mouffe, supra n. 95. Conceiving the world as politically constituted by peoples requires respect for each country’s economic, social, cultural and historical background, at the same time conferring upon its members (directly or indirectly) both the autonomy to engage in democratic contestation and the power to shape the rules of the international (tax) game.

In such a frame of mind, those concerned with justice and development in the world must focus on finding new paths and strategies that can bolster the capabilities, leverage and bargaining powers of weaker states, reducing the gap in resources that separates them from the developed world.
See L. van Apeldoorn, BEPS, Tax Sovereignty and Global Justice, 21 Crit. Rev. Intl. Soc. & Pol. Phil. 4, pp. 16-17 (2016), criticizing Dietsch and Rixen’s proposal for disregarding distributional patterns among countries, and concluding that “while the OECD’s ostensible concern with effective tax sovereignty is laudable, ... the structure of [BEPS] proposals insufficiently addresses the injustice of current inequality in effective tax sovereignty. [...] [T]he principle of economic allegiance on its own does not protect states in their quest for FDI from tax competition (‘real’ tax competition) that may undermine their capacity to engage in redistributive programmes. [...] The principle of economic allegiance (which allocates tax jurisdiction in accordance with the economic activity of MNCs) is non-responsive to the distribution of fiscal self-determination between countries. The countries where MNCs conduct economic activities are not necessarily the countries that have low fiscal self-determination. Accordingly the OECD’s dependence on the principle of economic allegiance is misguided. [...] [R]evised principles of tax justice, based on an internationalist conception of justice that is concerned with securing the effective sovereignty of independent polities, may need to prescribe the creation of globally redistributive institutions”; Dagan, supra n. 53, at p. 10: “cooperation alone is no guarantee of justice and ... certain transfer payments between rich and poor countries might be required to ensure this”; Stewart, supra n. 118, at p. 309: “If we are to take the idea of international redistribution seriously, we therefore need to unpack the very concept of the “country” (or state) that has at its core the ability to tax and redistribute”. Only by doing so will these countries be able to challenge the structures in place and demand mitigation of global disparities.

section.title: Conclusion
section.p: Moving in the direction of a more legitimate world tax order requires a direct confrontation with the asymmetry of power between states, as well as the problematic powerful global actors including international institutions, private groups and experts. These actors have been, for many decades, shaping tax law and policy to the benefit of a few through technical discourses behind closed doors. Owing to these entrenched power structures, it is not clear that an ITO, even if nominally inclusive, could make a real contribution to improving the position of marginalized countries in international tax policymaking.

Instead of trying to derive substantive principles of international taxation and enforcing them by a supranational institution, the initiative should start from the bottom up, critically evaluating the current practices of global tax governance, so as to find ways to make them more attuned with democratic principles. For a genuine multilateral democratic order to become a reality, it is imperative to continue pushing towards a multipolar and plural world that is open to the formation of several autonomous regional blocs, reversing the concentration of political power, thereby decentralizing and diffusing authority in the global arena.

In the end, what would be desirable is the establishment of strong local and regional platforms for political articulation, resistance and contestation, where developing countries could truly engage with each other in a collaborative effort to advance their fiscal and economic interests. That way, they would be able to demand more profound reforms in the ITR, directed towards global equality and socioeconomic development. Through regional integration, South-South cooperation, and the like, these countries might be able to exercise their political voice, in order to promote a more progressive and pro-development tax agenda than the one driven by the OECD, providing support for alternative policies on how to deal, in a more equitable and fair way, with tax base erosion.

This is certainly not an easy task. But it is a necessary first step for developing countries to be able to achieve their emancipatory and transformative goals. As long as they remain excluded from the processes of tax policymaking that take place on the global stage, they will never be able to shake up the status quo, which tilts the playing field decidedly in favour of countries in the “Global North” and perpetuates centuries-old inequalities and hierarchies.
